stages.surface: First ~13 km tarmac climb out of Vinstra, then gravel forest and mountain roads (~55% unpaved)
stages.summary: Morning train Oslo S to Vinstra on the Dovre line (~3h20, dep ~08:00, arr ~11:30), then a short but steep afternoon opener: 950 m of climbing past Fefor up to ~1,025 m before dropping into Skabu at 870 m.
highlights: Crossing the Gudbrandsdalslagen on the cycle bridge to start the route
Fefor Hoifjellshotell and Feforvatnet, the first taste of mountain scenery
First gravel of the trip through pine forest with swimmable lakes
Skabu, one of Norway's highest farming villages, with campsite and groceries
stages.notes: Book bike spaces on the Oslo-Vinstra train well in advance at entur.no (limited spots, ~5 services/day). Buy lunch in Vinstra before rolling out; Skabu has a Joker grocery for dinner/breakfast. Climb is front-loaded: sections over 8%. Skabu campsite or cabins; wild camping legal 150 m from buildings.

stages.surface: ~80% gravel: Jotunheimvegen toll road (45 km) along the Vinstre lakes; final 12 km tarmac on Fv51 from Bygdin
stages.summary: The signature Mjolkevegen day: the 45 km Jotunheimvegen gravel toll road from Skabu past Olstappen and along the Vinstre lakes to Bygdin at 1,065 m, then a fast 12 km on Fv51 down to Beitostolen.
highlights: Jotunheimvegen: car-toll gravel road with sparse, slow traffic (bikes free)
Rolling high plateau past stolar (summer dairy farms) that give the Milk Road its name
Lake Vinstre with the Jotunheimen peaks rising behind
Bygdin Fjellhotell cafe and the historic M/B Bitihorn boat on Lake Bygdin
Beitostolen ski village: restaurants, supermarkets, sports shops, campsite
stages.notes: No resupply between Skabu and Bygdin (~45 km), carry lunch. Surface is well-compacted gravel but exposed and weather-shifty above 1,000 m. Beitostolen is the last full-size resupply before the Besseggen detour: tomorrow is Saturday, shops open; Sunday everything closes.

stages.surface: 100% tarmac: Fv51 Valdresflye National Tourist Route
stages.summary: Detour day off the Mjolkevegen: north past Bygdin and over the Valdresflye pass at 1,389 m (one of Norway's highest paved roads), then a 400 m descent to Gjendesheim at the east end of Lake Gjende.
highlights: Valdresflye summit (1,389 m): treeless high plateau with full Jotunheimen panorama
Rjupa rest area and the National Tourist Route architecture
Descent with views over emerald Lake Gjende
Gjendesheim DNT lodge, classic Norwegian mountain hospitality
Short day: afternoon free for a leg-stretch up Besshoe foothills or a swim
stages.notes: Buy two days of snacks in Beitostolen this morning (Sunday closures tomorrow); Gjendesheim DNT serves dinner, breakfast and packed lunches. Book Gjendesheim beds AND tomorrow's 0845-ish Gjendebaten boat to Memurubu in advance at gjende.no, July weekends sell out. Tent pitches available near the lodge.

stages.surface: Hike: rocky marked mountain trail (14 km, ~1,100 m ascent). Ride: 33 km tarmac back over Valdresflye
stages.summary: Besseggen day: 20-minute boat to Memurubu, hike the ridge back to Gjendesheim (14 km, 6-8 h, ~1,100 m up), then an evening ride back south over Valdresflye to Beitostolen in the long July daylight.
highlights: Gjendebaten boat ride along Lake Gjende to Memurubu
Besseggen ridge: the classic view of green Gjende 400 m directly below blue Bessvatnet
Veslfjellet summit at 1,743 m
Sunset-lit return ride over the Valdresflye plateau (sunset ~22:30)
stages.notes: It is Sunday 12 July: groceries closed everywhere, so run on yesterday's supplies plus lodge food (Memurubu has a cafe). The ridge has easy scrambling; fine in trainers in good weather, skip in rain/high wind. The evening 33 km ride (+450 m) is the price of a comfortable schedule; alternative is a second Gjendesheim night, then ride the full Rallarvegen on the final day and lose the Finse overnight and Flam half-day.

stages.surface: ~60% gravel: Slettefjellvegen gravel toll road, tarmac through Vang/Ryfoss valley, gravel and short singletrack over Syndisfjellet
stages.summary: The hardest and most spectacular Mjolkevegen stage: the Slettefjell toll road climbs at a steady 5-8% to 1,295 m, plunges 600 m into Vang, then climbs again over Syndisfjellet past the Syndin lakes to Vaset.
highlights: Slettefjellvegen summit plateau at 1,295 m, the visual high point of the Mjolkevegen
600 m descent in ~6 km into the Vang valley
Ryfoss village for mid-ride resupply
Syndinstoga mountain canteen: waffles and hot chocolate
Syndin lakes and stolar pastureland into Vaset
stages.notes: Resupply in Beitostolen before leaving (Monday, shops open). 1,600 m is the biggest climbing day of the trip; start early. Slettefjellvegen is a toll road for cars, free for bikes. Vaset has Vaset Fjellcamping, cabins and hotels; small grocery nearby. Watch for cows with calves on the open pasture roads.

stages.surface: ~70% gravel: stolsveg gravel across Stolsvidda and Golsfjellet, short singletrack segments, tarmac descent into Gol
stages.summary: Gentle pastoral riding across the Stolsvidda plateau west of Fv51, past Tisleifjorden and Lykkja to the Golsfjellet ski areas, then a long descent off the plateau to Gol, the Mjolkevegen terminus.
highlights: Stolsvidda: gravel through Norway's densest summer-farm landscape, fresh dairy stops
Tisleifjorden lake and Tisleidalen
Wide singletrack through pine forest with dams and small cafes
Storefjell Resort and Oset hotels on Golsfjellet (lunch options)
Riverside gravel run into Gol; full-service town with supermarkets and outdoor shops
stages.notes: Officially the most family-friendly Mjolkevegen section: a recovery day after Slettefjell. Gol marks the end of Mjolkevegen signage. Stock up in Gol (sports shop sells dehydrated meals) and consider pre-booking the optional Geilo-Haugastol train for tomorrow. Gol campsite or cheap hotel.

stages.surface: Tarmac cycle path and gravel river-side tracks (National Cycle Route 4) Gol-Geilo; 22 km tarmac on Rv7 Geilo-Haugastol (train alternative)
stages.summary: The connector: National Cycle Route 4 up Hallingdal through Torpo, Al and Hol to Geilo (climbing from 207 m to 794 m), then the trafficked Rv7 to Haugastol at 988 m, gateway to the Rallarvegen.
highlights: Gravel track on the south bank of the Hallingdalselva, criss-crossing the Bergen railway
Torpo stave church (12th century) just off route
Al and Geilo for cafe stops and final big resupply
Ustedalsfjorden lakeside at Geilo
Arrival at Haugastol Turistsenter, km 0 of the Rallarvegen
stages.notes: Longest day, but the gradient is mostly gentle valley riding. The 22 km Rv7 section Geilo-Haugastol is fast and has no cycle path; local advice is to take the train or bus for that hop, book bike space ahead (entur.no). Buy any dehydrated meals/spares in Geilo: last proper shops before Flam. Haugastol has cabins, camping and a cafe.

stages.surface: 100% gravel: Rallarvegen navvies' road, car-free after the first kilometres
stages.summary: Onto the Rallarvegen proper: 27 traffic-free gravel kilometres climbing gently from 988 m to Finse at 1,222 m, Norway's highest railway station, beneath the Hardangerjokulen glacier.
highlights: Car-free riding along the historic Bergen railway construction road
Snowfields and turquoise meltwater lakes even in mid-July
Finse 1222: highest station in Norway, no road access
Rallarmuseet navvies museum at Finse
Hardangerjokulen glacier views (Hoth in The Empire Strikes Back)
Short day: time for the museum and a glacier-view walk
stages.notes: Deliberately short stage after yesterday's 80 km: Finse is worth the afternoon. Book Finse 1222 hotel or DNT Finsehytta well in advance (dinner+breakfast+packed lunch packages); tenting by Finsevatnet is the budget option, exposed at 1,200 m, pack for near-freezing nights. Rallarvegen officially opens in July; check snow status at haugastol.no/rallarvegen.com, early-season drifts may mean short hike-a-bike.

stages.surface: Gravel, rough and rocky in sections past Hallingskeid and Kleivagjelet; walk the 21 switchbacks on Myrdalsberget; asphalt for the final ~10 km of Flamsdalen
stages.summary: Over the route's 1,343 m high point at Fagervatn, then a 1,300 m net descent past Hallingskeid and Myrdal, down the 21 hairpins of Myrdalsberget into Flamsdalen and along the river to the fjord at Flam. Arrive early afternoon for fjord time.
highlights: Fagernut vokterbolig (1,310 m), the highest line-inspector's cottage in Norway, now a cafe
High point of the whole trip at Fagervatn, 1,343 m
Wooden bridges over crashing meltwater rivers towards Hallingskeid
Myrdalsberget: 21 switchbacks dropping ~300 m to Kardal (walk them)
Kjosfossen and the waterfalls of Flamsdalen alongside the Flam Railway
Flam on the Aurlandsfjord: afternoon fjord cruise to the UNESCO Naeroyfjord, kayaking, or the Aegir brewpub
stages.notes: Dismount for the 21 hairpins: official guidance warns of brake overheating on the loose, steep surface; stretch not recommended for under-12s. Several exposed, unsecured sections and patchy phone coverage between Finse and Myrdal: carry wind/waterproofs. Book Flam accommodation ahead (cruise-ship town, very busy). Return options: Flamsbana to Myrdal + Bergensbanen to Oslo (reserve bike space at vy.no) or express boat to Bergen.

sources: https://bikepacking.com/routes/of-milk-and-navvies/ (full route guide: 383 km, 7,282 m, 71% unpaved; stage km/ascent for Ruten-Beitostolen 72 km/1,211 m, Beitostolen-Vaset 65 km/1,601 m, Vaset-Gol 51 km/765 m, Gol-Haugastol 80 km/1,490 m, Haugastol-Flam 82 km/1,195 m; Rv7 warning and train alternative)
https://www.valdres.no/opplevelser/aktiviteter/sykling/mjolkevegen (official: ~250 km Vinstra-Gol, National Cycle Route 5, Jotunheimvegen and Stolsvidda pasture plateaus, GPX/map downloads)

https://www.valdres.com/things-to-do/activities/cycling/mjolkevegen/route-descriptions-and-recommendations (official stage suggestions: Beitostolen-Vaset via Slettefjell ~70 km 'hardest, most spectacular'; Vaset-Golsfjellet ~35 km 'most family friendly')

https://de.valdres.com/sehen-und-erleben/mjolkevegen-vinstra-jotunheimvegen-beitostolen-p3391413 (official stage figures: Vinstra-Skabu 27.5 km/950 m; Haugseter-Bygdin 14 km/220 m; Bygdin-Beitostolen 12 km/150 m)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jotunheimvegen (45 km gravel toll road Skabu-Bygdin, open late June-October, bikes free)

https://www.haugastol.no/en/rallarvegen (official distances: Haugastol-Finse 26 km, Finse-Hallingskeid 19 km, Hallingskeid-Vatnahalsen 17 km, Vatnahalsen-Flam 16 km, total 78.4 km; high point Fagervatn 1,343-1,350 m, Fagernut 1,310 m; Finse 1,222 m; 21 switchbacks to Kardal, dismount advised; season July-late September)
https://www.nasjonaleturistveger.no/en/routes/valdresflye (Fv51 Valdresflye National Tourist Route, 49 km Garli-Hindsater, summit 1,389 m)
https://gjende.no/en/walk-besseggen/ (Gjendebaten Gjendesheim-Memurubu ~20 min, up to 7 daily departures, pre-booking required)
https://www.geilo.com/en/tour-suggestions-bike (Geilo-Al 28 km on NCR4; train/bus recommended Geilo-Haugastol due to Rv7 traffic)

food.name: Restaurant Skadir (Skåbu Fjellhotell)
food.description: The post-ride dinner in Skåbu. Boutique mountain-hotel restaurant doing local game, mountain trout and produce from the surrounding fells. Verified open to non-guests ('open to everyone, both hotel guests and visitors'); lunch is walk-in but dinner must be reserved by 12:00 the same day (+47 61 29 55 00), so call before you roll out of Vinstra. Runs an ice cream kiosk in summer (confirmed on visitskabu.no). Active site, menus and booking for 2026.
food.price: Mountain-hotel a la carte; budget ~350-500 NOK pp for dinner (estimate, prices not published)

food.name: Coop Marked Skåbu
food.description: The village store and your last proper resupply before the high country toward Beitostølen. Coop Marked at Skåbuvegen 2666 in the Krogenæs building, with post-in-shop and pharmacy outlet (confirmed). Hours per kassal.app: Mon-Thu 09-18, Fri 09-20, Sat 09-18, Sun 12-16; another aggregator shows Mon-Thu 09-17 and Fri 09-19, so treat weekday closing as 17-18 and verify. Sunday 12-16 opening confirmed by multiple listings, rare for Norway, so a Sunday passage is survivable.

food.name: Krogenæsbua
food.description: Seasonal pop-up cafe and village info point in the same Krogenæs building as the Coop, partly self-service. Confirmed on visitskabu.no as open through the 2025 summer season (until autumn break). No published confirmation yet for summer 2026 and no fixed hours, so treat as a bonus stop, not a planned meal; the Coop next door is the fallback.

food.name: Skåbu Høyfjellsmat
food.description: Local food producer run by the fjellhotell, with a 2026 price list: dry-cured and smoked elk, wild-lamb sausages and fenalår, mountain-pork sausages/bacon/ham, elk burgers and soup, smoked whitefish, blueberry jam. Vacuum-packed cured sausage is ideal pannier protein for the two thin-services days ahead. Sold from the hotel at Skåbuvegen 2724 in the village centre; order ahead via post@skabuhoyfjellsmat.no or +47 61 29 55 00.

sleep.name: Wild camp: east shore of Lake Olstappen (near the Vinstra outlet dam)
sleep.description: Pine-fringed shoreline on Olstappen (662 m, confirmed), about 2 km south of the village and directly on the next day's line toward Jotunheimvegen, so you lose no distance. The Vinstra river outlet and dam at the lake's NE corner are confirmed; open forest shore with swimming nearby. Pitch south of the dam, away from the scattered cabins, and keep the 150 m allemannsretten distance. Warmer night than camping up on the 870 m plateau. Open-fire ban applies 15 Apr-15 Sep.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking; just keep 150 m from inhabited houses/cabins and leave no trace.

sleep.name: Skåbu Hytter og Camping
sleep.description: The village campsite at Skåbuvegen 2313, ~3.5 km before the centre as you climb in from Vinstra (coordinates verified against the address). Tent pitches plus 7 cabins (from basic 4-bunk huts at 600 NOK to a 7-person cabin with bathroom at 1,950 NOK; 2026 prices confirmed on their site). Open year-round per their own website, so running in mid-July. New hosts (Oline and Sigurd) since autumn 2025. Listed tent price is car+tent 275 NOK (+50 for power); cyclists with a small tent may pay less, ask.
sleep.price: Tent ~275 NOK; cabins 600-1,950 NOK (2026 prices confirmed)
sleep.bookingNote: Tent pitches are walk-up friendly even in July; book cabins ahead (skabucamping@gmail.com / +47 417 66 900 or +47 477 51 404) as there are only seven.

sleep.name: Skåbu Hyttegrend
sleep.description: Cabin village and campsite on Lake Olstappen, ~7 km southwest of the centre at the start of Jotunheimvegen, i.e. already on the next day's route to Beitostølen. Location confirmed on OSM and listed as open year-round on TripAdvisor, ACSI, Campio and trivago, with cabins, tent pitches, boats and canoes. Caution: its own website (skabu-hyttegrend.no) was unreachable during verification in June 2026, so confirm by phone before relying on it. Good choice if you want to bank kilometres past the village before stopping.
sleep.price: Tent ~250-300 NOK; furnished cabins roughly 800-1,500 NOK (unconfirmed, verify when booking)
sleep.bookingNote: Small operation and its website was down at time of checking: call ahead in mid-July (+47 904 11 004; a Campio listing shows +47 468 75 619 as an alternative). Have Skåbu Hytter og Camping as backup.

sleep.name: Skåbu Fjellhotell
sleep.description: Boutique hotel in the village centre of Norway's highest permanently inhabited village: 12 renovated doubles, 3 apartments, 2 suites (inventory confirmed), all with private facilities, plus Restaurant Skadir downstairs. The comfortable night if the weather turns; breakfast, wifi and parking included. Active website and 2026 bookings on aggregators confirmed.
sleep.price: Doubles from ~$211 (~2,100 NOK) on aggregators, i.e. roughly 1,800-2,400 NOK incl breakfast; confirm direct
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July: only 12 doubles and it is the only hotel for 20 km. +47 61 29 55 00 / booking@skabufjellhotell.no.

towns.notes: Skåbu is the natural first night out of Vinstra (~20 km but ~600 m of climbing). Key logistics: (1) Coop Marked Skåbu opens Sundays 12-16 (confirmed by multiple listings), but you will most likely pass on Thu 9 or Fri 10 July anyway; stock 1.5-2 days of food here because services westward are thin until Beitostølen. (2) Dinner at the hotel's Restaurant Skadir requires reservation by 12:00 the same day (confirmed on hotel site). (3) Next staffed food/bed on the route west is Haugseter Fjellstue on Jotunheimvegen by the Vinstre lakes (haugseter.no), worth pre-booking; the DNT self-service cabin Storhøliseter is off-route in Espedalen, ~1 hr detour (not re-verified here). (4) Use visitskabu.no for current village info; skabu.com was serving normal local directory content when checked in June 2026, so the earlier 'domain hijacked' claim looks wrong, but visitskabu.no is the maintained portal. (5) Skåbu Hyttegrend's own website was unreachable at verification time: call before counting on it, and keep Skåbu Hytter og Camping (open year-round) as the fallback.
towns.verificationNotes: Removed: nothing. All 4 food and 4 sleep entries verified as real and operating (or plausibly operating) in July 2026. Fixed: (1) Skåbu Høyfjellsmat source URL: skabuhoyfjellsmat.no now 301-redirects to skabufjellhotell.no/matopplevelser/skabu-hoyfjellsmat; shop address confirmed as Skåbuvegen 2724, which geocodes to exactly the listed coords (the hotel building); added 2026 price list confirmation and contact email. (2) Skåbu Hytter og Camping: season corrected from '1 June-30 Sept' to open year-round per their own site; added second phone and new-hosts note; lng refined 9.4577 to 9.4580 per address geocode. (3) Skåbu Fjellhotell price hint raised from 'from ~$178' to 'from ~$211' per 2026 aggregator rates (~1,800-2,400 NOK). (4) Wild camp: distance corrected from ~3 km to ~2 km south of the village (geodesic from village node 61.5256,9.4094); lake elevation 662 m, NE-corner Vinstra outlet and dam all confirmed via Wikipedia. (5) Notes: removed the 'skabu.com domain hijacked' claim, which did not hold up (site served normal local directory pages when checked). Flagged but kept: Skåbu Hyttegrend, whose own website refused connections twice during checking; existence and year-round operation corroborated by OSM (coords exact match), TripAdvisor, ACSI, Campio and trivago, so kept with a strengthened call-ahead caveat and unconfirmed-price flag. Krogenæsbua kept with existing caveat: confirmed for the 2025 season on visitskabu.no, 2026 not yet published. Coordinates: Coop (Skåbuvegen 2666), camping (Skåbuvegen 2313), hotel/Skadir/Høyfjellsmat (OSM hotel node) and Hyttegrend (OSM camp_site node) all match geocoded positions to within ~30 m; no coordinate errors found. Coop hours in the data match the cited kassal.app source exactly, including the unusual Sunday 12-16 opening; one other aggregator shows Mon-Thu 09-17 / Fri 09-19, so the verify-on-arrival caveat was extended to weekday closing times.

food.name: Heimatt Bakeri & Restaurant
food.description: The village bakery, run by two brothers from a local Beitostolen farm who also run the 10-seat farm-to-table 'Heilt Heimatt' tasting restaurant and Heimatt catering (ranked #3 in town on TripAdvisor). Cinnamon buns get rave reviews, some call them the best in Norway. Bygdinvegen 3782, mid-village (in the Bergo building area). Bakery operates in summer but with a smaller range than winter; restaurant runs Wed-Sat and books out. Bakery hours are short and posted on their Facebook page, so check before relying on it for breakfast.
food.price: Bakery items NOK 40-90; restaurant dinner NOK 600+

food.name: Cafeen Tapasbar
food.description: Cozy cafe-bar 'full of soul' on the main drag (Bygdinvegen 3802): bistro dishes, sharing plates and tapas, homemade focus. Good relaxed dinner for two riders. Hours verified: Mon-Thu 15:00-22:00, Fri-Sat 12:00-23:00, CLOSED Sundays; kitchen until 22:00. Sister wine bar Chambre by Cafeen next door.

food.name: Bryggerihuset Beitostolen
food.description: Highest-rated spot in town (TripAdvisor; Yelp listing updated May 2026): brewpub at Bygdinvegen 3776 with the widest beer selection in Beitostolen, gastropub food, and outdoor summer grilling. Note it sits ~200 m SE of the SPAR/roundabout cluster, on the lower stretch of Bygdinvegen. The obvious post-ride beer stop. Verify summer opening days on their Facebook/Instagram; it leans weekend-heavy outside ski season. Tel +47 924 48 646.

food.name: Jotunstogo
food.description: Traditional Norwegian cafe-restaurant-winebar by the Rv51 roundabout (Bygdinvegen 3783, next to SPAR), known for its Today's Special, generous portions, fast service and mountain views; they will wrap leftovers to take away. Open daily 10:00-18:00 (Sun 11:00-18:00), so it works for breakfast, lunch or an early dinner, and is one of the few places serving on Sundays. Closes 18:00, so eat early.

food.name: SPAR Beitostolen
food.description: Main resupply: full supermarket at Bygdinvegen 3783 with fresh bakery, produce, hot/cold deli counter, in-store Vitusapotek pharmacy and Post i Butikk. Open Mon-Sat 08:00-22:00 and, unusually for Norway, SUNDAYS 09:00-20:00 (tourist-resort exemption, verified). Backup options in town: KIWI at Ovrevegen 4A (closed Sundays) and Coop Extra at Stolstunet, Bygdinvegen 3762. Last proper supermarket before the Slettefjell climb to Vaset.

sleep.name: Tutjerne tarns wild camp (plateau above Beitostolen)
sleep.description: Cluster of small swimmable tarns at ~1,050 m on the open plateau 2.5-3 km NE of the village, below Knausehogda (1,123 m) and Melbyfjellet. Location verified against Kartverket's official place-name register ('Tutjerne', group of tarns, Oystre Slidre, 61.2636 N 8.9267 E). Reached via Ovrevegen/Gronasvegen and a marked, checkpoint-numbered trail network from the centre (the Knausehogda-Tutjern loop is ~6.5 km; rideable gravel most of the way, short steep push at the start). Above the treeline on uncultivated stolsvidde well over 150 m from the nearest cabins, so allemannsretten applies. Popular day-hike picnic/swim spot with a rest area: arrive in the evening, pitch discreetly away from the trail, pack out everything.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking. Max 2 nights in one spot under allemannsretten; expect wind and possible boggy ground after rain.

sleep.name: First Camp Beitostolen - Valdres (ex Beitostolen Hytter & Camping)
sleep.description: Official campsite at Finntoppvegen 2A, ~1.2 km SE of the centre on the way in from Fagernes, within walking distance of restaurants and shops. Open year-round (verified on First Camp's live site), taken over by the First Camp chain. Cabins plus tent/van pitches, service buildings, sauna, laundry, kitchen, mini-golf, outdoor chess, playground; hiking trails start from the site. Address geocodes exactly to the listed coordinates.
sleep.price: Tent pitch ~NOK 350-500 for two; cabins from ~NOK 1,000-1,500
sleep.bookingNote: Tent pitches normally fine on arrival; cabins should be booked ahead for mid-July (Norwegian fellesferie starts then). Contact: beitostolen@firstcamp.no.

sleep.name: Bergo Hotell / Bergo Rom
sleep.description: Cheapest proper beds in the village: simple hotel rooms, apartments and cabins at Bygdinvegen 3782, dead centre, 100 m from SPAR and the restaurants. Doubles (incl. family rooms with sofa beds) listed from roughly USD 81-94 a night on aggregators (Kayak from $94, Planet of Hotels from $81), i.e. ~NOK 850-1,300. Many rooms face Slettefjell. Good drying/recovery option if the weather turns. Tel +47 61 35 10 00.
sleep.price: Double from ~NOK 850-1,300
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July, especially Fri/Sat: Beitostolen is a busy summer hiking/cycling resort and the cheap rooms go first.

towns.notes: Beitostolen is a full ski-resort town at 900 m and the best resupply point between Vinstra and Gol: supermarkets (SPAR open Sundays 09:00-20:00, rare in Norway; KIWI and Coop Extra Mon-Sat), sports shops (Intersport, Sport 1, Sporten Beito) for tubes/spares, in-store pharmacy at SPAR, and a dozen restaurants clustered along ~600 m of Bygdinvegen. Caveats: (1) several restaurants close Sundays (e.g. Cafeen Tapasbar), but Jotunstogo is open Sundays 11:00-18:00; some places run reduced days outside ski season, so check Facebook/Instagram pages for July hours. (2) If you hit Sunday 12 July further down the route, most groceries in Vaset/Gol will be shut while Beitostolen SPAR is open. (3) The next leg over Slettefjellvegen (1,315 m) to Vaset has no resupply, so load up here. Heimatt bakery runs a reduced summer range and short hours; confirm on their Facebook page before relying on it for breakfast.
towns.verificationNotes: All 5 food and 3 sleep entries verified as existing and summer-operating; nothing removed. Checks: (a) existence confirmed via live listings (Visit Beitostolen, beito.com, TripAdvisor Dec 2025 rankings, Yelp updated May 2026, spar.no store page, en.firstcamp.se, Kayak/Booking, Kartverket place-name register, OSM/Nominatim); (b) coordinates cross-checked against Nominatim geocoding and Kartverket. Fixes: Bryggerihuset had the largest error, moved ~200 m from 61.2482,8.9068 to its OSM position 61.2471,8.9096 (Bygdinvegen 3776 is SE of the roundabout cluster, not next to Bergo); Cafeen Tapasbar moved ~110 m south to the geocoded Bygdinvegen 3802 address point (61.2487,8.9052); Heimatt moved ~70 m to its OSM POI (61.2481,8.9074); Bergo, Jotunstogo and SPAR nudged under 50 m to OSM POI positions. Tutjerne wild camp coordinates matched Kartverket's official register exactly (61.2636,8.9267) and First Camp matched the Finntoppvegen 2A geocode exactly. (c) Seasonality: First Camp confirmed year-round; Heimatt bakery confirmed operating in summer (reduced range); Bryggerihuset shows 2026 activity but summer weekday hours remain a check-on-arrival item (caveat retained). (d) Prices sane: Bergo doubles from $81-94 on aggregators (widened hint to NOK 850-1,300); Jotunstogo NOK 200-300 pp per Restaurant Guru (replaced the NOK 180-250 special guess). Detail upgrades: Jotunstogo hours verified as daily 10:00-18:00 incl. Sundays 11:00-18:00 (confirms the 18:00 close and makes it the Sunday-dinner fallback); Cafeen Tapasbar hours and Sunday closure confirmed exactly as stated; SPAR Sunday 09:00-20:00 confirmed; swapped two sources to more authoritative pages (Visit Beitostolen for Cafeen and Bryggerihuset).

food.name: Café Gjendeosen
food.description: Café at the Gjende boat pier, 200m from the DNT lodge. Freshly baked waffles, cinnamon buns and bread, plus warm dishes, ice cream and snacks. Opens one hour before the first Gjendebåten departure and closes an hour after the last boat: 06:45-21:00 in July high season, seven days a week, so the Sunday problem does not apply here.
food.price: Waffles/buns ~50-90 NOK, light meals ~100-200 NOK (estimate; no published price list)

food.name: Gjendesheim Turisthytte (DNT dinner)
food.description: The big staffed DNT lodge at the foot of Besseggen serves a proper three-course dinner built on local produce, with summer sittings at 19:00 and 21:00, plus breakfast. The best post-ride dinner at the stop itself. Note: DNT staffed lodges do not allow eating your own food or using stoves on the premises (confirmed in 2026 price list). Open daily through July; summer season runs roughly early/mid-June to early October.
food.price: 3-course dinner 470 NOK member / 608 NOK non-member; breakfast 190/253 NOK (official 2026 DNT Oslo og Omegn prices)

food.name: Bessheim Fjellstue restaurant
food.description: Family-run mountain lodge about 5 km north of Gjendesheim down Sjodalen (paved Fv51, easy spin). Well regarded kitchen: three-course dinner served daily through the summer season (5 June to 20 September in 2026), mountain trout a speciality. Open daily in July including Sundays. Dinner must be reserved by the day before: phone +47 61 23 89 13 or post@bessheim.no.
food.price: 3-course dinner ~570 NOK (2025 reference; 2026 not published); breakfast ~210 NOK

sleep.name: Wild camp at Nedre Leirungen (below Knutshøe)
sleep.description: Classic legal wild-camp under allemannsretten: grassy shores of lake Nedre Leirungen in Leirungsdalen, beneath the Knutshøe ridge, reached by a short walk/push from Fv51 on Valdresflye about 4 km south of Gjendesheim (roadside pull-offs). Open uncultivated mountain terrain, far more than 150m from any building, with Gjende and Knutshøe views; a known photographers' camping spot. Note tents are explicitly not allowed in the Gjendeosen/Reinsvangen parking lots, so camp here or on the heath above the lodge instead. Coordinates are the lake centroid; pitch anywhere suitable on its shores.
sleep.price: Free (optional: pay 125 NOK at the DNT lodge for shower/facility access as a 'telter')
sleep.bookingNote: No booking; arrive before dusk for flat pitches, expect wind on the plateau

sleep.name: Besseggen Fjellpark Maurvangen
sleep.description: The official campsite for the area (Hoskelivegen 27), about 4 km from Gjendesheim by the Sjoa river at ~1000m. Tent pitches plus rental cabins, modern bathrooms, guest kitchen, on-site café, sauna and fire pits. Open year-round, check-in from 15:00. They guarantee arrivals by tent a spot, so it is the safe fallback in peak July.
sleep.price: Hiking tent incl. 2 persons 390 NOK/night in peak season (13 June-16 Aug); extra person 40 NOK; shower 5 NOK/min; cabins from ~400 to ~1650 NOK by size (book online)
sleep.bookingNote: Tent: no booking needed (spot guaranteed). Cabins: book well ahead for mid-July, Besseggen peak season

sleep.name: Gjendesheim Turisthytte (DNT)
sleep.description: DNT's flagship staffed lodge (since 1878), ~185 beds in 1-, 2- and 4-bed rooms and dormitories, right at the Gjende boat pier and Besseggen trailhead. Showers included, linen rental available (sleeping bag liner required, rental 114-130 NOK). Summer season roughly early/mid-June to early October, operating 2026. The most atmospheric option and the only bed at the stop itself.
sleep.price: Dorm bed 290 NOK member / 382 non-member; 4-6 bed room 445/578; 1-3 bed room 515/670; full board (DNT pensjon) 1095-1315 member / 1430-1710 non-member (official 2026 prices)
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July (most visited lodge north of the Alps) at hyttebestilling.dnt.no; DNT tradition means nobody is turned away, worst case a mattress on the floor (186/300 NOK, only when all beds are taken)

sleep.name: Bessheim Fjellstue og Hytter
sleep.description: Mountain lodge hotel about 5 km north of Gjendesheim overlooking Øvre Sjodalsvatnet: 27 rooms (64 beds, made beds, simple standard) plus 27 cabins/apartments (116 beds) with kitchenettes, sauna, canoe rental and a good restaurant. Summer season 5 June to 20 September 2026, on the Fv51 National Scenic Route. A comfortable cheap-hotel alternative if the DNT lodge is full.
sleep.price: Rooms from ~1100 NOK; cabins ~1850 NOK; breakfast ~210 NOK (2025 reference prices; confirm on booking)
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July via post@bessheim.no or +47 61 23 89 13; also on Booking.com

towns.notes: Gjendesheim is a trailhead hamlet, not a village: one DNT lodge, one pier café, zero shops. Resupply fully at Kiwi Beitostølen (confirmed open Sundays 09:00-21:00) before crossing Valdresflye. All listed places confirmed operating with 2026 seasons: DNT lodge summer season early/mid-June to early October with dinner sittings 19:00 and 21:00; Bessheim 5 June-20 September with dinner reservation required the day before; Maurvangen year-round; Café Gjendeosen 06:45-21:00 in high season tied to the boat schedule. Mid-July is Besseggen peak: book the DNT lodge or Bessheim ahead; tent options need no booking. DNT prices are now the published official 2026 DNT Oslo og Omegn list (not estimates). All coordinates verified against OpenStreetMap/Nominatim to four decimal places.
towns.verificationNotes: Removed: nothing. All 8 entries exist, are operating, and are open mid-July. Verified via official sites (visitgjende.no, bessheim.no, maurvangen.no, dnt.no) plus OSM Nominatim for every coordinate. Coordinates: all 6 locations matched OSM nodes exactly (Café Gjendeosen 61.49539/8.80924, DNT lodge 61.49435/8.81264, Bessheim 61.51462/8.85297, Maurvangen 61.48826/8.84549, Kiwi 61.24287/8.92095, Nedre Leirungen lake centroid 61.47600/8.79543); no coordinate fixes needed. Fixes applied: (1) DNT prices updated from 2025 to the now-published official 2026 list (dorm 290/382 vs old 280/370, 4-6 bed 445/578, dinner 470/608, breakfast 190/253, floor mattress 186/300, full board 1095-1710), so the notes' '~5% uplift expected' caveat is gone. (2) Bessheim distance corrected from 7 km to ~5 km (bessheim.no states 5 km from the boat; straight-line ~3.1 km). (3) Bessheim dinner: site requires table reservation the day before, added; exact 2025 dinner window '14 June-20 August, 18:30-20:30' replaced with confirmed 2026 season 5 June-20 September. (4) Maurvangen tent price corrected: peak-season hiking tent incl. 2 persons is 390 NOK (old hint 250-350 was low); cabin range kept as unverified estimate flagged for online booking. (5) Café Gjendeosen prices marked as estimates (no published price list; hours 06:45-21:00 high season confirmed). (6) DNT season softened to 'early/mid-June to early October' (sources vary between ~6 June and mid-June opening; immaterial for mid-July). Unverified but plausible and flagged: Bessheim room/dinner kroner amounts (2025 references), Maurvangen cabin range, café price estimates.

food.name: Vasetstølen
food.description: Historic stølsgard (summer-farm courtyard, 1700s/1800s log buildings) serving the best traditional food at Vaset: rømmegrøt is the signature (up to 50L served daily, order it with cured meats like the locals), plus majel soup, mountain trout, local lamb and game. Season runs 1 June to 10 Oct. Summer serving hours confirmed on the official site (June 2026): Tue-Thu 12:00-16:00, Fri-Sat 12:00-17:00, Sun 12:00-18:00, closed Mondays. Open Sunday 12 July. Cyclist-friendly, listed Mjølkevegen partner. Note: occasionally closes for private events (e.g. confirmations), so a quick call (+47 61 36 33 50) the day before is cheap insurance.

food.name: Sekskanten Kro og Pub
food.description: The post-ride dinner option at Vaset centre: hexagonal kro/pub with daily a la carte, dish of the day, burgers, and traditional Valdres dishes (rømmegrøt, majel). Dine in or takeaway. Hours confirmed on official site (June 2026): Mon-Fri 11:00-17:00, Sat 12:00-19:00, Sun 12:00-17:00, so eat early; kitchen closes by 17:00 most days. No seasonal closure listed. Confirmed operating (sekskanten.no, +47 61 34 47 00 / 415 08 415).

food.name: Joker Vaset (Vasetsenteret)
food.description: Main resupply for this leg and an unusually good one: named store of the year in the Joker chain (award covered by Avisa Valdres), stocks local seasonal produce plus hardware/sports goods, and is a confirmed sales outlet for Bakeriet i Valdres (Bakeriet i Bagn) baked goods. Critically, it is OPEN SUNDAYS 11:00-17:00 (cabin-area exemption), so Sunday 12 July resupply works here. Mon-Thu/Sat 09:00-18:00, Fri 09:00-21:00. Address Panoramavegen 901, 2960 Røn. Also the check-in point for Vaset Camping (same phone/email: 61 36 30 55, joker.vaset@joker.no).
food.price: Norwegian grocery prices (Joker is ~10-15% above Kiwi/Rema)

sleep.name: Tisleifjorden northwest shore (wild camp, Stølsvidda)
sleep.description: Open mountain pasture (utmark) along the Mjølkevegen gravel road at the north end of Tisleifjorden (lake centre geocodes to 60.856, 8.947), roughly 1-2 km south of Langestølen. Treeless stølsvidda at ~820m with flat grassy shoreline pitches and lake water. Legal under allemannsretten: stay 150m+ from the stølar and cabins clustered at Langestølen, max 2 nights, and note Norway's general open-fire ban runs to 15 Sep. Riding 13 km past Vaset to camp here shortens the next day's stage to Gol. Closer alternative if you want to stay at Vaset itself: the lakeshore at Vasetvatnet is mostly ringed by cabins, so the paid campsite is the better option in the village.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking. Carry water treatment or boil; lake and stream water.

sleep.name: Vaset Camping (Vasetsenteret)
sleep.description: Family-run campsite directly behind the Joker/Vasetsenteret at Vaset centre, ~800m altitude. Accepts tents plus ~70 motorhome pitches; showers (extra charge), toilets, guest kitchen (oven, 2 hotplates), laundry, WiFi. Check-in via the Joker supermarket (shared phone 61 36 30 55 and email joker.vaset@joker.no), which keeps long hours including Sunday. No rental cabins at the campsite itself (the similarly named Vasetdansen Camping with cabins is a different site in Tisleidalen, off your route, do not confuse them). Confirmed open year-round (1 Jan-31 Dec listing).
sleep.price: Tent pitch roughly 150-250 NOK (an older camper report cites 100 NOK/night, so expect modest), showers extra
sleep.bookingNote: Drop-in fine for tents, even in mid-July; check in at Joker Vaset.

sleep.name: Vasetstølen Hytter
sleep.description: Eight fully equipped cabins sleeping 4-9 persons each (per the official site, June 2026; older listings said nine cabins/65 beds), set in the historic farm courtyard near Vasetvatnet with the traditional restaurant on site, so dinner and breakfast are sorted without riding anywhere. Listed Mjølkevegen accommodation; cyclists explicitly welcomed. Open 1 June to 10 Oct. The charm pick if you want a roof and the best food at Vaset in one stop.
sleep.price: Price on request (small cabins typically ~1,200-1,800 NOK); phone +47 61 36 33 50 or +47 91 15 57 95
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July (Norwegian holiday season); cabins sell out on summer weekends.

sleep.name: Gomobu Fjellstue
sleep.description: Family-owned mountain lodge at ~1,000m above Vaset on Valtjednvegen (toll road, ~40 NOK access fee, card accepted), 36 refurbished rooms plus 10 large cabins (3 bedrooms, private sauna). Official Mjølkevegen partner with ski/bike storage and bike rental. Restaurant serves traditional Norwegian dinner with local ingredients; non-guests can eat if they book ahead. Eco-Lighthouse certified. The comfort option: hot meal, sauna, real bed. Bookable on Booking.com with 2026 rates live.
sleep.price: ~1,400-2,000 NOK per double incl breakfast
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July; it fills with hikers and Mjølkevegen riders. +47 61 35 77 10.

towns.notes: Vaset is a small mountain resort hamlet (Vestre Slidre, ~810m) at the end of the Beitostølen-Slettefjell-Vaset 70 km Mjølkevegen stage, so you will likely land here on Sat 11 or Sun 12 July. Key Sunday fact: Joker Vaset is open Sundays 11:00-17:00 (cabin-area exemption from Norway's Sunday closing law), so a 12 July resupply works. Vasetstølen restaurant is open Sundays 12:00-18:00 but closed Mondays; Sekskanten closes at 17:00 on Sundays, so plan an early dinner or self-cater. Kitchens generally close early at Vaset, arrive by mid-afternoon if you want a sit-down meal. Vasetstølen occasionally closes for private events, so call ahead. Beware the name trap: Vasetdansen Camping is a different site in Tisleidalen, not at Vaset.
towns.verificationNotes: Adversarial check, June 2026. All 4 food and 4 sleep entries pass existence, location, season, and price checks; nothing removed. Coordinates verified against OpenStreetMap/Nominatim and match to 4 decimal places for Vasetstølen (60.99341, 8.97735), Sekskanten (60.99545, 8.98533), Joker Vaset (60.99607, 8.98903), Gomobu (60.98069, 8.98593); Langestølen verified via its street address Tisleivegen 1680, 2918 Ulnes geocoding to 60.87664, 8.94153 (lng corrected 8.9417 to 8.9415, trivial); wild-camp point sits correctly between Langestølen and the Tisleifjorden lake centroid (60.856, 8.947). Season/hours verified on primary sites: vasetstolen.no currently shows exactly the claimed Tue-Sun hours with Mondays absent and season 1 Jun-10 Oct confirmed via Visit Norway; sekskanten.no shows the claimed hours with no seasonal closure; Joker Sunday 11-17 and weekday hours confirmed; Vaset Camping confirmed year-round, tents accepted, no cabins, check-in/contact via Joker. 12 July 2026 is indeed a Sunday. Fixes applied: (1) Vasetstølen Hytter corrected from nine cabins/65 beds to eight cabins sleeping 4-9 each per the current official site; (2) Vaset Camping price hint reframed to ~150-250 NOK per tent (only hard datapoint is a 100 NOK/night camper report from 2017, the claimed 100-150 NOK per person was unsourced); (3) Langestølen description now flags that summer hours are unpublished (open winter holiday, Easter, plus ~4 summer months per Visit Norway/Valdres listings) rather than implying confirmed July hours; (4) added caveat that Vasetstølen occasionally closes for private events (its own site shows a June 2026 Sunday closure for a confirmation); (5) Gomobu toll-fee wording softened to ~40 NOK (road and toll confirmed as Valtjednvegen, exact fee not independently verified) and 36 rooms/10 cabins/partner status/phone confirmed via valdres.com. Bonus claims verified: Joker store-of-the-year award (Avisa Valdres) and Bakeriet i Valdres outlet at Joker Vaset (bakerietivaldres.no) are both real. Vasetdansen Camping is confirmed as a separate site in Tisleidalen; kept the warning but dropped the unverified 19 km figure.

food.name: Gol Bakeri og Konditori
food.description: Traditional Hallingdal bakery with two outlets in town (main at Sentrumsvegen 11, branch at Sentrumsvegen 114). Old-school methods, big counter of fresh bread, cakes, buns and ice cream; lots of seating, ~80 seats at the main outlet. The obvious morning stop before rolling out toward Geilo. Hours (verified on golbakeri.no): main Mon-Fri 09:00-16:00, Sat 09:00-15:00; branch Mon-Fri 09:00-17:00, Sat 09:00-16:00. BOTH CLOSED SUNDAYS, so no bakery breakfast on 12 July.

food.name: Kjersti's Mat og Vinhus
food.description: The best sit-down food in Gol centre (Sentrumsvegen 65). Cafe by day (soups, sandwiches, salads, cakes baked in-house daily), a la carte in the evening with a dry-aging cabinet for steaks; summer terrace. Tripadvisor 3.8/5, #2 of 17 in Gol; reviews praise the dry-aged entrecote, some find portions small for the price. WARNING: listed hours are Mon-Thu 11:00-20:00, Fri-Sat 11:00-22:00, i.e. CLOSED SUNDAYS. Do not plan on it for a Sunday 12 July dinner; call +47 954 84 216 to check for summer exceptions.

food.name: Erik's Grill & Sportsbar (Pers Hotell)
food.description: Informal grill and sports bar inside the Pers Hotell complex, Sentrumsvegen 72 (some listings give Dalevegen 1, same building block). Burgers including an Impossible Burger, a la carte grill menu, beer on tap, Premier League and F1 on screens, piano bar with live music on weekends. Best bet for a post-ride dinner on a Sunday evening when much else in Gol is shut (hotel kitchen, but exact Sunday hours not published; the hotel's main restaurant serves dinner daily as fallback). Note: bar and grill have a 20+ age minimum, children only when accompanying adults.

food.name: Bistro at First Camp Gol - Hallingdal
food.description: On-site bistro at the First Camp campsite (Heradvegen 189), indoor and outdoor seating, rustic simple meals from snacks to spareribs and plaice, beer and wine. Open during reception hours (typically 08:00-19:00 outside peak weeks), so plan an early dinner. Only worth it if you sleep at First Camp; saves the 3 km ride back into the centre. Site and bistro confirmed operating for the summer 2026 season.

food.name: Kiwi Gol
food.description: Main resupply point, Sentrumsvegen 96 at the west end of the centre (on your way out toward Geilo). Confirmed OPEN SUNDAYS 09:00-21:00 (tourist-town exemption), so the Sunday 12 July closure problem does not bite in Gol. Weekdays 07:00-23:00, Sat 09:00-21:00. Gol is the biggest shopping town between Fagernes and Geilo: stock up here. There is also an Extra supermarket at Sentrumsvegen 128 as backup.

sleep.name: Wild camp: Golsfjellet heather plateau near Kamben
sleep.description: The Hallingdal valley floor around Gol is fenced farmland (innmark), so legal wild camping in town is effectively impossible. Instead, stop on the open heather-and-birch plateau around Kamben on Golsfjellet (Kamben hamlet verified at 60.802, 8.979), just off the Mjolkevegen gravel road about 10 km / 550 vertical metres before the final descent into Gol. Utmark, allemannsretten applies; pitch 150 m+ off the road and away from the Kamben hamlet buildings and stol (summer farm) fences. Small tarns and streams for water, big views back over the plateau. Bikepacking.com's 'Of Milk and Navvies' route (Mjolkevegen + Rallarvegen) passes this exact stretch into Gol. Then it is a fast freewheel into Gol; remember the bakery is shut if it is Sunday.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking. Avoid pitching inside summer-farm grazing enclosures; livestock roam freely up here.

sleep.name: Personbråten Camping
sleep.description: Small family-run campsite on the bank of the Hallingdalselva (Rotneimsvegen 48), confirmed open year-round (ACSI: 1 Jan-31 Dec). 15 min walk to the centre and on the WEST side of town, i.e. zero backtrack when you leave for Geilo. 60-70 pitches with power; river swimming on site and Gol's main Mobrua swimming spot 120 m away. Sources conflict on huts: norcamp says no cabins, visitnorway mentions a few mobile bungalows for rent, so treat it as tent/van only. Some road and railway noise. GPS verified against norcamp listing.
sleep.price: Solo tent likely ~150-250 NOK; ACSI comparison price ~€34/night for pitch + 2 adults + car; van pitch 350 NOK incl. power (Aug 2025)
sleep.bookingNote: No advance booking needed for a tent, even mid-July; turn up and pitch. Phone +47 915 51 429 if in doubt.

sleep.name: First Camp Gol - Hallingdal
sleep.description: Big full-service chain campsite at Heradvegen 189 (coordinates verified), 3 km EAST of the centre along the Rv7 (slight backtrack from your direction of travel). Tent pitches plus a large stock of cabins and apartments, on-site bistro, mini-market, jumping pillow, and the Hallingstampen outdoor pool with waterslide confirmed reopening for summer 2026 (renovated 2024). Summer weeks 27-32 have included family activities and a 'stay 3, pay for 2' promotion. The comfort option if you want a cabin night and a swim.
sleep.price: Tent pitch ~350-450 NOK (estimate); cabins from ~900-1,800 NOK in high season (book for exact rates, not published on site)
sleep.bookingNote: Cabins should be booked ahead for mid-July (Norwegian summer weeks 27-32 are peak); tent pitches are normally available but can be pre-booked with pitch selection on firstcamp.no. Contact gol@firstcamp.no / +47 32 07 41 44.

sleep.name: Pers Hotell
sleep.description: Three-star family resort hotel right in the centre (Sentrumsvegen 72, coordinates verified). Not fancy but comfortable: pool/waterpark area, breakfast included (buffet gets consistently good reviews), secure bike storage on request, and Erik's Grill & Sportsbar plus the main restaurant downstairs for dinner. The realistic hotel night in Gol; the town has no hostel or DNT hut.
sleep.price: Double from roughly 1,600-2,500 NOK/night incl. breakfast in July (aggregators show from ~USD 159-260)
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July; Norwegian fellesferie (industrial holiday) starts around 10 July and Gol fills with family traffic.

towns.notes: Gol is the end of Mjolkevegen and the best resupply town on the whole route between Fagernes and Geilo. Key Sunday 12 July facts: Kiwi Gol is open Sundays 09:00-21:00 (confirmed via kiwi.no store listing), Gol Bakeri (both outlets) and Kjersti's Mat og Vinhus are closed Sundays per their published hours, so Sunday dinner means Erik's Grill / Pers Hotell restaurant or the First Camp bistro (early hours). Routing note: Personbråten Camping and Kiwi sit on the west side of town (direction Geilo), First Camp Gol is a 3 km backtrack east. All coordinates re-verified against OpenStreetMap Nominatim geocoding of the street addresses; wild-camp coordinates are an indicative pitch area on open utmark near Kamben (hamlet verified at 60.802, 8.979), not a facility.
towns.verificationNotes: Adversarial check, June 2026, all 9 entries verified as existing and operating; none removed. Method: web search per venue plus Nominatim geocoding of every street address. Coordinate results: all five geocodable addresses (Sentrumsvegen 11, 65, 72, 96; Heradvegen 189) matched the supplied lat/lng to 4 decimal places, and Personbråten's coords match the norcamp GPS listing exactly, so no coordinate fixes needed. Kamben hamlet confirmed at 60.802, 8.979, consistent with the indicative wild-camp point. Fixes made: (1) Kjersti's, original said 'confirm Sunday hours by phone'; golinfo lists hours as Mon-Thu 11-20, Fri-Sat 11-22 with no Sunday service, so flagged as closed Sundays, do not rely on it for 12 July. (2) Pers Hotell price raised from 1,100-1,700 NOK to ~1,600-2,500 NOK: aggregators (Kayak, Planet of Hotels, Trip.com) show July doubles from USD 159-260. (3) Personbråten, 'no cabins' softened to a noted source conflict (visitnorway mentions mobile bungalows, norcamp says none) and tent price widened to ~150-250 NOK against the ACSI comparison price of €34 for pitch+2+car; year-round operation confirmed via ACSI (1 Jan-31 Dec). (4) Erik's Grill, added the 20+ age rule for bar/grill from golinfo and noted Sunday hours are unpublished; some listings give Dalevegen 1, same Pers Hotell block, coords unchanged. (5) First Camp bistro, noted it runs on reception hours (~08-19 outside peak), so dinner is early; Hallingstampen reopening for summer 2026 independently confirmed on firstcamp.se. (6) Gol Bakeri hours and the second outlet (Sentrumsvegen 114) confirmed on golbakeri.no/utsalg; both outlets closed Sundays as originally stated. Bikepacking.com 'Of Milk and Navvies' route exists and passes Golsfjellet into Gol. Kiwi Sunday hours 09:00-21:00 confirmed. Sources: golbakeri.no, golinfo.no, kiwi.no, firstcamp.se, eurocampings.co.uk (ACSI), norcamp.de, visitnorway.com, Tripadvisor, Kayak/Trip.com aggregators, bikepacking.com, nominatim.openstreetmap.org.

food.name: Haugastol Turistsenter cafe & restaurant
food.description: The hub at the start of Rallarvegen and effectively the only food at Haugastol itself: coffee bar, restaurant and bike shop in one family-run complex by the station. Tripadvisor reviewers rate the food among the best they've had in Norway: fresh meat dishes, good salad/veg choices, praised pizza and a big spread of homemade cakes. Restaurant serves breakfast, lunch and dinner mid-June to mid-October; in the 1 July-9 August window breakfast is 08:00-10:00 and dinner is served daily at 19:00, Sundays included, so mid-July is fully covered. Dinner ~390 NOK, breakfast ~175 NOK (approximate; exact prices only shown in booking flow).

food.name: Joker Ustaoset
food.description: The resupply point. Haugastol has no grocery store, so this Joker at Ustaoset station (Ustaosvegen, by the rail line), 11 km east of Haugastol on your riding line from Geilo, is the last proper shop before Finse/Flam. Crucially it is open Sundays 11:00-18:00 (verified), which matters with 12 July 2026 falling on a Sunday: do not arrive before 11:00 expecting it open. Weekdays 10:00-18:00 (Fri to 20:00), Sat 09:00-18:00. Stocks daily groceries, propane, OTC medicines and local Hallingdal products.

food.name: Fagerheim Fjellstugu cafe
food.description: Staffed mountain lodge on Rv7 southwest of Haugastol (9 km direct, ~12 km by road) on the edge of Hardangervidda with views toward the Hardangerjokulen glacier. Cafe open 11:00-18:00 every day, year-round (confirmed on the lodge's own site and ut.no), plus a set three-course dinner served 19:00. A worthwhile detour ride for dinner if Haugastol's restaurant is full, or a meal stop if you camp on the plateau nearby. Note: no consuming your own food on the premises.
food.price: Three-course dinner included for overnight guests; cafe a la carte

food.name: Oksebotn rallarcafe (vokterbolig)
food.description: Historic 1913 railway guard's house ~19 km along Rallarvegen toward Finse (8 km before Finse), at ~1,150 m. Tiny privately run summer cafe famous for homemade hot waffles: longtime waffle-maker Eivind Haukvik turns out 120-150 waffle plates a day on busy weekends. Normally opens daily from mid-July (snow-dependent) until Norwegian school start, hours sporadic. Perfect mid-ride stop on your Haugastol-Finse leg, but with a mid-July trip you may arrive days before it opens for the season: carry food.

sleep.name: Haugastol Turistsenter (apartments + mountain rooms)
sleep.description: The obvious roof at Haugastol: modern 4-bed (Type A/B) and 8-bed (Type D) apartments with kitchens, plus two budget shared 'mountain rooms' (8 bed spaces total) for those happy to share. Restaurant, coffee bar, bike shop and Rallarvegen bike rental on site; full-service mode (reception + meals) through July, with half-board available as an add-on for mountain rooms. Note: sleeping bags are not permitted in the mountain rooms, bedding rents on arrival.
sleep.price: Rates shown only in booking flow; apartments roughly 1,500-2,500 NOK/unit, mountain room is the budget option. Dinner ~390 NOK, breakfast ~175 NOK
sleep.bookingNote: Mid-July is peak Rallarvegen season and this is the route's main start point: book weeks ahead via haugastol.no or +47 32 08 75 64.

sleep.name: Fagerheim Fjellstugu
sleep.description: Family-run fjellstugu on Rv7, ~12 km by road southwest of Haugastol (a 30-40 min ride with loaded bikes, off the Rallarvegen line but an easy out-and-back). Dorm bed 850 NOK pp or double room 1,050 NOK pp, both including made bed, towels, breakfast AND a three-course dinner at 19:00: strong value for hungry riders versus Haugastol. DNT members get 5% off accommodation. Open year-round (verified on official site, including holidays), operational for 2026.
sleep.price: Dorm 850 NOK pp, double 1,050 NOK pp, half board included
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July. Cancellation: free more than 14 days out, 50% charge 8-14 days out, 100% charge within 7 days of arrival. Check-in 15:00-18:00 (call +47 922 58 498 if later). Note: no self-catering allowed at Fagerheim.

sleep.name: Wild camp: open heath off Rv7 toward Fagerheim
sleep.description: Open, treeless heath with lakes and streams on the Hardangervidda edge, ~4 km southwest of Haugastol beside Rv7 toward Fagerheim. Flat tent-friendly ground, water from lakes and streams, and legal under allemannsretten provided you stay 150 m+ from the scattered cabins (tents are explicitly not permitted in the cabin fields immediately around Haugastol and Finse). Critical caveat: do NOT camp along the first 13 km of Rallarvegen from Haugastol toward Finse or around Finse itself, that corridor is off-limits, so pitch south of Rv7, not on the cycle route.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking; exposed at 1,000m+ so expect wind and possible single-digit temps even in July.

towns.notes: Haugastol (1,000 m) is a railway-stop hamlet, not a village: one tourist centre, no grocery store, no independent restaurants. Plan accordingly. (1) Resupply: buy food at Joker Ustaoset 11 km east as you ride in from Geilo; it opens 11:00-18:00 on Sundays (relevant since 12 July 2026 is a Sunday), and it is the last shop until Flam. (2) Wild camping is banned on the first 13 km of Rallarvegen from Haugastol toward Finse, around Finse, and in the cabin fields immediately around Haugastol (per Cycle Norway's route guide), so free-camp south of Rv7 instead, or push past the 13 km mark next morning. (3) There is no official campsite at Haugastol; the nearest is back in Geilo, your previous stop. (4) Haugastol Turistsenter runs full service with restaurant and bike shop in July (dinner served daily at 19:00 from 1 July) and books out in Rallarvegen peak season; reserve early or take Fagerheim Fjellstugu's half-board dorm at 850 NOK pp (~12 km by road southwest), which beats most DNT pricing. (5) Snow can linger on the Haugastol-Finse section into mid-July; check haugastol.no/en/rallarvegen-report before riding, and don't count on the Oksebotn waffle cafe being open yet, it normally starts mid-July.
towns.verificationNotes: Adversarial check, June 2026. Nothing removed: all 4 food and 3 sleep entries verified as real, operating, and summer-open. Fixes applied: (1) Oksebotn rallarcafe, the most suspicious entry (easily confused with the Fagernut cafe west of Finse), confirmed as a distinct, real waffle cafe at the Oksebotn guard house ~19 km from Haugastol / 8 km before Finse via ut.no (hytte 10824), norwaybybike.com and Ostlendingen press coverage (waffle-maker Eivind Haukvik, 120-150 plates/day, normally open daily mid-July to school start); coordinates corrected to Kartverket's official Oksabotnen point (60.5592, 7.5969, was 60.5587, 7.599) and elevation adjusted 1,160 m to ~1,150 m. Added stronger warning that a mid-July arrival may precede its seasonal opening. (2) Fagerheim Fjellstugu: coordinates exact per OSM; prices (dorm 850 / double 1,050 NOK pp incl. half board), DNT 5% discount, no self-catering, and year-round opening all confirmed on the official site; cancellation policy corrected (original understated it: actual is 50% charge 8-14 days out and 100% within 7 days); distance corrected from '9 km, 15-20 min ride' to ~12 km by road, 30-40 min with loaded bikes. (3) Haugastol Turistsenter: coordinates confirmed by OSM hotel node (60.5124, 7.8531); restaurant season confirmed (meals mid-June to mid-Oct, dinner daily 19:00 from 1 July per haugastol.no Q&A); apartment types (4 and 8 beds) and mountain-room rules (no sleeping bags, bedding rental, half-board add-on) confirmed on accommodation page; exact 390/175 NOK meal prices not found on public pages so flagged approximate, but they are plausible for Norway. (4) Joker Ustaoset: coordinates exact per OSM; Sunday 11:00-18:00 opening confirmed, added the 11:00 Sunday opening time since the trip hits a Sunday. (5) Camping ban on first 13 km toward Finse and around Finse confirmed via Cycle Norway; also applies to cabin fields around Haugastol itself, added. Unverifiable river name 'Usteane' at the camp spot removed (Kartverket places Usteane east of Ustaoset); spot renamed and described generically, coordinates kept as plausible open heath south of Rv7.

food.name: Finsehytta cafe & restaurant (DNT)
food.description: DNT's big staffed lodge on the south shore of Finsevatnet, 300m from the station. Daytime cafe does Norwegian waffles, cakes, pizza and lighter hot meals; evenings a proper 3-course dinner. Home of Finse 1223, Norway's highest microbrewery (in the cellar), with their own pilsner, pale ale and cider on tap. Open to non-residents, daily through the summer season; 2026 summer season opens 26 June, so fully open mid-July. Renovated and expanded in recent years.
food.price: Waffles/cake ~60-100 NOK, pizza ~200 NOK, 3-course dinner ~545 NOK (DNT price list dated 2023, expect modest increases)

food.name: Hotel Finse 1222 restaurant & bar
food.description: Historic railway hotel right on the platform. Restaurant does locally sourced 3-course dinners (rotating set menu) with glacier views, plus a bar/lounge for a post-ride beer. Lunch (homemade pizza, reindeer burger, Rallarsuppe) is served daily but must be booked in advance. Open to non-residents. Summer season covers mid-July, and from 2026 the hotel runs year-round.
food.price: 3-course dinner roughly 600-700 NOK; bar prices typical Norwegian mountain hotel

food.name: Fagernut Vokterbolig cafe (Rallarvaffel stop)
food.description: Stone track-watchman's cottage from 1904 at 1,310m, 9km west of Finse directly on Rallarvegen, run by the Navvy Museum. THE classic stop on the Finse-Myrdal leg: waffles, coffee and navvy soup. CAUTION on dates: opens daily 10:00-16:30 from roughly MID-July (exact date varies with snow clearing) until school start, then weekends to late September. On a 14-16 July ride it may have just opened or not yet; check the Rallarvegen report and do not rely on it for calories. Cash in NOK or Vipps; card not always accepted.

food.name: No grocery store at Finse (resupply in Geilo)
food.description: Finse has zero shops: no Kiwi/Joker/Coop, nothing. Last proper supermarkets before Finse are in Geilo (Kiwi, Spar, Coop Extra), with a small shop at Ustaoset and a kiosk at Haugastol Turistsenter. Buy stove food and snacks in Geilo, and remember 12 July 2026 is a Sunday when Geilo groceries are largely shut (small sondagsapen exceptions aside). The lodges at Finse sell some chocolate/snacks but nothing you could cook from. Next real shop after Finse is in Flam.

sleep.name: Wild camp: south shore of Finsevatnet (across the dam)
sleep.description: The one sanctioned wild-camping zone at Finse. Camping is only allowed on the south shore of Finsevatnet because the lake is the settlement's drinking-water supply; signs at Finse mark where you can pitch. Cross the small dam at the lake outlet near Finsehytta and pick a grassy flat along the shore west of the hut, well away from cabins. Flat-ish, watery, exposed at 1,200m; expect mosquitoes in fine weather and possible snow patches even mid-July. Standard allemannsretten rules, max 2 nights. Bonus: walk back to Finsehytta for dinner and a Finse 1223 beer.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking; arrive and pitch. Bring a 4-season-ish setup, nights can hit low single digits in July.

sleep.name: Finsehytta (DNT staffed lodge)
sleep.description: Large staffed DNT lodge (sleeps 200+ including overflow mattresses), 300m from the station across the lake outlet. Bunk rooms and dorms, shared showers, drying room, and the full-board package includes breakfast, packed lunch and a 3-course dinner: ideal rider fuel. On-site microbrewery. The closest thing to a campsite-with-services Finse has; there is no official campsite in the village. 2026 summer season opens 26 June.
sleep.price: Full board (bed + breakfast + 3-course dinner + lunch + shower): dorm 1,060 NOK DNT member / 1,390 NOK non-member; rooms 1,200-1,275 / 1,580-1,660 NOK pp. Price list dated 2023, expect modest increases.
sleep.bookingNote: Book ahead for mid-July via hyttebestilling.dnt.no, Rallarvegen peak season. DNT members are never turned away even when full (mattress on the floor). A DNT membership pays for itself across a multi-hut trip.

sleep.name: Hotel Finse 1222
sleep.description: The historic ~44-room railway hotel on the platform (where parts of The Empire Strikes Back were filmed). Sauna, bike rental and workshop, drying facilities, restaurant and bar. The splurge option after days in a tent. Open through summer and going year-round from 2026.
sleep.price: Roughly 3,000-3,500 NOK per night for two including 3-course dinner, breakfast buffet and packed lunch (recent guests report ~USD 325/night packages).
sleep.bookingNote: Book well ahead for mid-July; it is the only hotel at Finse and fills with Rallarvegen cyclists and hikers.

towns.notes: Finse is car-free, reachable only by rail or by riding Rallarvegen, and has NO grocery store: carry dinner-gap food from Geilo (Kiwi/Spar/Coop Extra) and note 12 July 2026 is a Sunday when Geilo supermarkets are largely shut. At Finse itself the Sunday issue is moot since both lodges serve food daily. Timing caution: the Finse-Myrdal stretch of Rallarvegen is usually the last to clear of snow and officially opens around mid-July; arriving 14-16 July should work but check the Rallarvegen report (haugastol.no/en/rallarvegen-report) before committing, and be ready to walk short snow patches near Fagernut. The Fagernut cafe itself also only opens from roughly mid-July (snow-dependent), so treat it as a bonus stop, not a guaranteed resupply.
towns.verificationNotes: All 4 food and 3 sleep entries verified as real and operating; none removed. Fixes: (1) Hotel Finse 1222 coordinates corrected from 60.6025/7.5025 to OSM-verified 60.6018/7.5031 (both restaurant and hotel entries). (2) Fagernut opening dates corrected: haugastol.no states daily 10:00-16:30 from mid-July (snow-dependent) to school start, then weekends to late September, not 'early July'; added explicit warning it may not yet be open on a 14-16 July ride, and replaced the dubious geilo.com source URL with the haugastol.no Rallarvegen page. (3) Wild-camp entry: the restriction reason corrected from 'cabin owners' privacy' to the actual reason (Finsevatnet is the settlement's drinking-water supply, per local signage reported by multiple sources); the south-shore-across-the-dam rule itself confirmed by Wikivoyage. (4) Finsehytta bed count '174' could not be confirmed (Wikivoyage says 200+ with mattresses); softened. Confirmations: DNT price page confirms full-board dorm 1,060/1,390 NOK member/non-member and rooms 1,275/1,660 (price list dated Aug 2023, flagged as likely floor); Finsehytta 2026 summer season opens 26 June so mid-July is open; Finse 1223 microbrewery confirmed in the lodge cellar (RateBeer/Untappd); Hotel Finse 1222 year-round from 2026 confirmed (Visit Voss/Visit Norway), restaurant open to non-residents with advance booking needed for lunch; Finsehytta and Fagernut coordinates match OSM exactly; 12 July 2026 confirmed as a Sunday; grocery-advisory coordinates correctly point at Geilo center where the supermarkets are.

food.name: Flam Bakeri
food.description: Artisan bakery on the quay, steps from the Flamsbana station. Fresh skillingsboller (Norwegian cinnamon buns), bread baked through the day, sandwiches, pastries and Italian-style pizzas. Open every day year-round 08:00-17:00, so it works even on a Sunday. The obvious first stop after rolling off the Rallarvegen descent.

food.name: Aegir Gastropub (Flamsbrygga, formerly Aegir BrewPub)
food.description: Stave-church-styled brewpub on the harbourfront, long-running Tripadvisor #1 in Flam and multiple-time Norway BrewPub of the Year. The Jan 2026 renovation is complete: it reopened rebranded as Aegir Gastropub, one of three dining concepts at Flamsbrygga (alongside Fjord Bistro and Flam Restaurant). Viking-inspired menu with local beer pairings (the Viking Plank concept). Confirmed operating through summer 2026. Book a table in July.

food.name: Restaurant Arven (Fretheim Hotel)
food.description: The dining room of the historic Fretheim Hotel, 2nd floor: locally sourced fjord and farm ingredients, modern Norwegian cooking. High-season (May-Sept) evening buffet with fish, shellfish, local meats and desserts, around NOK 695 per person in recent reviews (expect a modest increase for 2026). The best 'proper' dinner in the village if you want to celebrate finishing the route. Reserve ahead in mid-July.

food.name: Fjord Bistro (formerly Furukroa Kafe)
food.description: Casual bistro-cafe in the harbour courtyard at Flamsbrygga, rebranded from Furukroa Kafe in the 2026 renovation. Lunch, dinner, barista coffee and cakes through the day; menu has included the Flam Burger, Aegir-beer-battered fish and chips, soup and pizzas, plus a summer ice cream counter. Big harbour-view terrace. The cheap-and-fast option when the bakery queue is long; busiest when cruise ships and trains coincide. Showing FIFA World Cup 2026 matches, so confirmed open in summer 2026.

food.name: Coop Marked Flam
food.description: The village grocery and your resupply point (chain: Coop). A-Feltvegen 18, near the cruise pier and train station, between the playground and the parking lot. Open Mon-Fri 08:00-20:00, Sat 09:00-18:00, and crucially Sun 12:00-18:00 (small-store exemption), one of the few Sunday-open groceries on the whole route. Stocks camping supplies. No alcohol sales on Sundays.

sleep.name: Flamselvi river flats below Berekvam (wild camp)
sleep.description: Don't wild camp in Flam itself: the valley floor around the village is farmland and built-up, and locals confirm camping around the village is not tolerated. Instead, stop short on your descent from Myrdal: grassy and gravel flats beside the Flamselvi around Berekvam (the Flamsbana passing-loop station, 343.8m, ~10.5km from Myrdal). This mid-valley stretch is uncultivated gorge between the farms at Kardal above and Hareina below, right on the Rallarvegen road. Keep 150m+ from the station buildings and any cabin, max two nights, leave no trace. Free under allemannsretten.
sleep.price: Free
sleep.bookingNote: No booking. Pitch late, leave early; the spot is beside the cycle road so expect passing riders in daytime.

sleep.name: Flam Camping & Hostel
sleep.description: Award-winning family-run campsite and HI hostel ~300m from the station, right where the Rallarvegen rolls into the village. ~80 tent pitches on terraced grass (some sheltered, power available, picnic tables and grills), hostel rooms, and cabins with and without bathroom; new sanitary block and kitchen completing for the 2026 season. Explicitly welcomes bikers and hikers; camp kitchen and laundry on site. This is the default bikepacker stop in Flam.
sleep.price: Tent NOK 400/night; hostel rooms from NOK 990 (shared bath) to NOK 1,200 (private); cabins from NOK 1,030 (2BR w/ bath NOK 2,800)
sleep.bookingNote: Mid-July is peak season in one of Norway's busiest tourist villages: book hostel rooms and cabins several weeks ahead. Tent pitches are more flexible but arrive by mid-afternoon.

sleep.name: Fretheim Hotel
sleep.description: The historic 122-room hotel at the head of the fjord, in business since the 1800s. Not cheap, but the end-of-trip splurge option: fjord-view rooms, big breakfast, Restaurant Arven upstairs, and secure bike storage on request. Recent guests report around NOK 2,800/night for a double; aggregator rates for peak season run higher, roughly USD 340-500.
sleep.price: ~NOK 2,800-4,500/night double in July
sleep.bookingNote: Book well ahead for mid-July; Flam sells out when cruise and tour traffic peaks.

towns.notes: Flam is the finish line of the route and one of Norway's most touristed villages: expect cruise-ship crowds midday, quiet evenings. Everything sits within 400m of the station (village centre 60.8625, 7.1144). Key practicalities: (1) Coop Marked is one of the few Sunday-open groceries on the entire route (Sun 12:00-18:00), though by your schedule you hit Flam around 16-17 July, not a Sunday. (2) Flamsbrygga's early-2026 renovation is finished: the dining venues now run as Aegir Gastropub, Fjord Bistro (ex-Furukroa) and Flam Restaurant, all confirmed operating summer 2026. (3) Wild camping in/around the village is effectively off the table (farmland plus mass tourism); camp up-valley near Berekvam on the descent or pay NOK 400 for a pitch at Flam Camping. (4) All accommodation: book ahead for mid-July, this is peak season at a pinch-point village.
towns.verificationNotes: All 8 entries confirmed to exist and operate in summer; none removed. Fixes: (1) Aegir BrewPub renamed: the Jan 2026 renovation (8-10 weeks) is complete and the venue reopened as 'Aegir Gastropub', one of three rebranded Flamsbrygga concepts; entry updated (flamsbrygga.no, visitnorway.com). (2) Furukroa Kafe was rebranded 'Fjord Bistro' in the same renovation (Visit Norway listing 4203 and Visit Sognefjord now show Fjord Bistro at the old Furukroa URL; flamsbrygga.no lists it as a current concept and is promoting FIFA World Cup 2026 screenings there, confirming summer 2026 operation); entry renamed with rebrand noted. (3) Coop Marked coordinates corrected from 60.8621,7.1145 to 60.8640,7.1160 (OSM/Mapcarta node: 60.864, 7.11605, near the cruise pier), ~230m off originally; Sunday 12:00-18:00 and weekday hours independently confirmed. (4) Fretheim Hotel price range widened to NOK 2,800-4,500 (guest reviews report NOK 2,800; Kayak/aggregators show ~USD 340-506/night in season, making the original NOK 2,400 floor optimistic for July). (5) Flam Camping prices verified exactly against flaam-camping.no (tent NOK 400, hostel from NOK 990, cabins NOK 1,030-2,800), including the new-for-2026 sanitary block and 'warm welcome to bikers and hikers'; removed the unverified 'bike rental on site' claim. (6) Flam Bakeri verified open daily year-round 08:00-17:00 (visitnorway.com, norwaysbest.com); coordinates plausible for the quay. (7) Restaurant Arven verified (Tripadvisor, norwaysbest.com): high-season buffet May-Sept, NOK 695 pp confirmed in reviews though it dates from recent seasons, caveat added. (8) Berekvam wild-camp coordinates sanity-checked against Wikipedia's station position (60.7883, 7.0957, elev 343.8m); the pitch coordinate sits ~150m away as intended, kept. Price hints otherwise plausible against Norwegian norms.

pois.name: Jotunheimvegen (Vinstre toll road)
pois.description: 45 km summer-only gravel toll road from Skåbu to Bygdin, built in the 1950s to replace the milk boat that carried dairy across lakes Sandvatnet and Vinstri: the literal origin of the 'milk road' theme. Easy, well-graded gravel along the lake shores with Jotunheimen's peaks growing on the horizon; toll applies to cars (120 kr in 2024), free for cyclists, open from late June. The Roros route rides its eastern stretch along Vinstre to Bygdin.
pois.detour: on route

pois.name: Lake Vinstre and Haugseter Fjellstue
pois.description: Jotunheimvegen runs along the northern shore of lake Vinstre (Vinstervatn, ~1030 m), a wide mountain lake dotted with old fishing hamlets and stølar. Haugseter Fjellstue mid-lake is the classic food-and-bed stop on this leg, 14 km before Bygdin; good swimming and trout fishing.
pois.detour: on route

pois.name: Bygdin quay and M/B Bitihorn
pois.description: Where Jotunheimvegen meets Fv51 at the east end of lake Bygdin (1060 m), beneath Bitihorn, with the historic Bygdin Høifjellshotell on the shore. M/B Bitihorn, in service since 1912 and the highest scheduled boat service in Northern Europe, sails the 25 km lake via Torfinnsbu to Eidsbugarden deep into southern Jotunheimen; two departures daily from late June, a worthwhile rest-day cruise.
pois.detour: on route; optional boat cruise ~3.5 h return to Eidsbugarden (1 h 45 each way)

pois.name: Bitihorn summit hike
pois.description: The 1607 m horn that dominates the Bygdin-Beitostølen skyline, with a moderately demanding walk-up (no scrambling, ~550 m gain, 4 km each way) and one of Valdres' best panoramas over lake Bygdin and the southern Jotunheimen 2000-ers. Trailhead parking is right on Fv51 about 10 km north of Beitostølen, so it slots straight into the riding day.
pois.detour: hike, 3-5 h return from Fv51 trailhead (on route)

pois.name: Synshorn Via Ferrata
pois.description: Popular via ferrata climbing iron rungs up the near-vertical face of Synshorn between lakes Bygdin and Vinstre to the ~1460 m summit. Guided trips run June-September, 3-4 h with ~400 m of climbing, no prior climbing experience needed (minimum age applies); book via synshorn.no.
pois.detour: guided activity, 3-4 h; start near Bygdin on route

pois.name: Rjupa rest area, Valdresflye
pois.description: Architect-designed rest stop (Knut Hjeltnes) on the Norwegian Scenic Route Valdresflye just above Bygdin, with a panorama over the old mountain hamlets along lake Vinstre and back across Valdres. Wheelchair accessible; also a trailhead for walks onto the plateau.
pois.detour: on route

pois.name: Flye 1389 (Valdresflye high point)
pois.description: The highest point of the Valdresflye scenic road at 1389 m (Norway's second-highest mountain pass), where Fv51 floats above the treeless plateau with views to Besshøe, Tjønnholstinden and Rasletinden. The modern Flye 1389 cafe at the top serves local food from Valdres and Gudbrandsdalen: a natural mid-climb reward on the ride to Gjendesheim.
pois.detour: on route

pois.name: Vargebakkane viewpoint
pois.description: Pull-off on the northern slope of Valdresflye (~1100 m) as the road drops toward Gjendesheim, with a head-on view of the 2000 m peaks, Knutshøe, lake Nedre Leirungen and the Besseggen ridge. Doubles as the Knutshøe trailhead (Vargbakken parking, ~4 km south of the Gjendesheim exit).
pois.detour: on route

pois.name: Knutshøe ridge hike
pois.description: The 'little sister of Besseggen': a 1517 m grassy-then-rocky ridge squeezed between lake Gjende and the Leirungen lakes, with some easy scrambling and big views over emerald Gjende. No boat or booking needed, far quieter than Besseggen; the ~13 km loop (690 m gain) starts at Vargbakken parking right on Fv51. Golden eagles nest here, so locals advise saving it for after 1 July (fine for mid-July).
pois.detour: hike, 4-6 h loop from Vargebakkane on Fv51

pois.name: Gjendesheim - Jotunheimen NP eastern gateway
pois.description: DNT's most visited staffed lodge north of the Alps (est. 1878, 156 beds), at ~1000 m on the east end of lake Gjende just off Fv51: the main eastern entry to Jotunheimen National Park, home to Norway's highest peaks and 60+ glaciers. Beds, hearty dinners and the Besseggen and Gjende boat trailheads make it the obvious overnight for a hiking day.
pois.detour: on route (end of the Fv51 Valdresflye leg)

pois.name: Lake Gjende and the Gjende boats
pois.description: An 18 km glacial lake famous for its vivid emerald-green colour from glacier-milled rock flour, often called Norway's most beautiful mountain lake. Passenger boats shuttle Gjendesheim-Memurubu-Gjendebu, with up to 7-8 departures daily to Memurubu in high season (the classic Besseggen approach); pre-book at gjende.no as July departures sell out.
pois.detour: on route at Gjendesheim; boat ~20-45 min each way

pois.name: Besseggen ridge
pois.description: Norway's most famous day hike (up to ~60,000 walkers/year): take the morning boat to Memurubu and walk the 14 km ridge back to Gjendesheim, climbing the dramatic crest between green Gjende and blue Bessvatnet, the two lakes separated by 400 vertical metres. Immortalised by Ibsen's Peer Gynt, who rode a reindeer buck along the ridge; allow a full day and book the boat well ahead for mid-July.
pois.detour: hike, 6-8 h plus boat to Memurubu (book gjende.no)

pois.name: Slettefjellet high point (1315 m)
pois.description: The highest point of the whole Mjølkevegen at 1315 m, reached by a stiff climb on Slettefjellvegen between Beitostølen and Vang (one of the route's most demanding sections); picnic spot at the top with a sweeping panorama back over the Jotunheimen massif before the steep descent toward Vang and Vangsmjøse. A Mjølkevegen rest shelter sits shortly before Mugnestølen partway along.
pois.detour: on route

pois.name: Høre stave church
pois.description: 12th-century stave church (timber dendro-dated 1179, matching a runic inscription about the Battle of Kalvskinnet) on the hillside above Ryfoss, passed on the steep descent from Slettefjell. One of Valdres' cluster of surviving stave churches, rich in medieval dragon and lion carvings; a two-minute culture stop between the day's two big climbs.
pois.detour: on route (just off the Slettefjell descent)

pois.name: Syndinstøga
pois.description: Mountain lodge and cafe on the Syndin lakes in the old stølsgrend above Ryfoss, a Mjølkevegen institution that began in 1963 by serving rømmegrøt and waffles in the farmhouse living room; today it has a shop, cafe and 22 beds, with rømmegrøt with cured meat still the signature dish. Ideally placed at the top of the steep Ryfoss-Syndin climb, ~20 km before Vaset.
pois.detour: on route (Syndin lakes)

pois.name: Lisjordetstølen (open summer farm)
