pois.description: Working mountain dairy on Stølsvidda, the area with the most active summer farms in Northern Europe, with 25 milking cows plus calves, goats, rabbits, chickens and a llama; open daily 12:00-18:00 throughout July with coffee and stølskost (simple farm food), and evening milking to watch 18:00-19:00 on selected days (advance sign-up, fills fast). On Panoramavegen at Rensenn between Vaset and Hemsedal: peak on-theme stop for a 'milk road' trip.
pois.detour: +15 km return from Vaset on Panoramavegen

pois.name: Stubbesetstølen (open summer farm)
pois.description: Old støl next to Vasetstølen on Panoramavegen at Vaset, open several days a week in summer: greet the animals and buy farm-made goat cheese, butter, lefse and cakes baked from old recipes in the farm bakery. The zero-detour option for stocking up on støl produce at the overnight stop.
pois.detour: on route at Vaset

pois.name: Langestølen Kolonial og Kafé
pois.description: Summer-season farm shop and cafe on Tisleivegen by Tisleifjorden in Tisleidalen, a popular stop for Mjølkevegen cyclists serving rømmegrøt and fresh waffles the traditional way; also sells homemade kuruv (Valdres cured sausage), pultost, butter and rømme, plus fishing permits and gear. Open four months over summer (plus winter holidays and Easter).
pois.detour: +13 km south of Vaset, or fold into the Vaset-Gol leg (it lies on the Mjølkevegen Vaset-Golsfjellet stage)

poiGroups.verificationNotes: All 20 POIs are real and in the right valleys; none removed. Every name, location and source theme was confirmed via independent web sources (Wikipedia, nasjonaleturistveger.no, valdres.com/visitnorway.com, DNT, gjende.no, jvb.no, operators' own sites). Corrections made: (1) Besseggen hiker count raised from ~30,000 to up to ~60,000/year, the figure used by the cited visitnorway.com source. (2) Lake Bygdin length fixed from 32 km to 25 km (Wikipedia); boat detour tightened to ~3.5 h return (1 h 45 each way, 2 departures/day), and the verified 'highest scheduled boat service in Northern Europe' added. (3) Bitihorn summit coordinate nudged ~0.8 km north to 61.301, 8.799 to match Peakbook/Wikipedia (61.30 N, 8.80 E). (4) Slettefjellet: removed the unsourced Murklopphøgda editorial note (Murklopphøgda is confirmed to be on Kongevegen over Filefjell, but no source cites it as Mjølkevegen's high point, so the correction corrected nothing) and the unverified '600 m climb in 6 km' figure; 1315 m as the route's highest point and the rest shelter before Mugnestølen are both confirmed by valdres.com. (5) Lisjordetstølen: 25 cows and daily 12:00-18:00 July opening confirmed on lisjordet.no, but milking viewing corrected to 18:00-19:00 on selected days with advance sign-up (was 'around 17:00-18:00' implied daily), food softened to 'coffee and stølskost' (waffles/lapper not confirmed), llama and chickens added. (6) Syndinstøga: '20+ beds' tightened to 22 (syndinstoga.no); 1963 founding and living-room rømmegrøt origin confirmed. (7) Langestølen: rømmegrøt 'served with kuruv' corrected - it serves rømmegrøt and waffles and sells kuruv/pultost/butter/rømme; opening corrected to 'four months over summer plus winter holidays and Easter'. (8) Synshorn: 'one of Norway's most popular via ferratas' softened to 'popular' (superlative unverified); synshorn.no booking, June-Sept season, 3-4 h and ~400 m climbing all confirmed; summit ~1460 m. (9) Per Gynt Gården: 'medieval farm' softened to 'historic' and the unverified claim that the route's first leg follows Peer Gynt-named roads removed; 17 listed buildings, 2.5 km from Vinstra and the Ibsen connection (Peder Olsen Hågå) confirmed. (10) Gjendesheim's 'most visited staffed lodge north of the Alps' superlative verified (jotunheimen.com/DNT), as were 1878, 1389 m Flye high point, Knutshøe 1517 m and ~13 km loop, Vargbakken parking at ~1100 m 4 km south of Gjendesheim, Rjupa by Knut Hjeltnes, Jotunheimvegen 45 km/1950s/milk-boat origin/late-June opening/120 kr car toll, Haugseter 14 km from Bygdin, Høre stave church 1179 dendro date, Langsua 537 km2/2011/Ormtjernkampen expansion with coordinate matching Wikipedia exactly (61°12'N 9°30'E), Hinøgslivegen rest shelter, Stubbesetstølen next to Vasetstølen, Stølsvidda 'most active summer farm area in Northern Europe', and Gjende boats up to 7-8 daily departures with pre-booking advice. Langsua source URL swapped to Wikipedia and Haugseter source to haugseter.no (better match for the claims made). Knutshøe gained a verified golden-eagle/post-1 July note, harmless for a mid-July trip. Coordinates for all other POIs sanity-checked against named lakes, roads and villages and found consistent.

pois.name: Lomen Stave Church
pois.description: Stave church from c. 1192 above the Slidre valley, with three lavishly carved portals, chancel arches and column capitals; open daily in summer with guides (2025 season: mid-June to mid-August, 10:00-18:00, adults NOK 130), exterior viewable year-round. The nearer-to-route Hegge stave church sits on the road up to Beitostolen on your previous leg, so Lomen is the Valdres stave church to target from Vaset.
pois.detour: ~22 km each way by road from Vaset (descend to Ron on Slidrefjorden, then E16 north-west); half-day round trip, easiest folded into the Beitostolen-Vaset day

pois.name: Stolsvidda summer farm plateau
pois.description: The Vaset-Golsfjellet stage crosses one of Norway's largest active summer-farming (stol) areas, a network of close to 200 stols with cows and sheep grazing free: summer-farm stops around Langestolen (cafe and small store), Jaslangen and Trollhovd, and long views back to Jotunheimen. This is the landscape that gives Mjolkevegen (the Milk Road) its name.
pois.detour: On route (the Vaset-Gol stage runs straight across it)

pois.name: Tisleifjorden
pois.description: Large regulated mountain lake (13.4 km2, 821 m) between Stolsvidda and Golsfjellet, the best swim-and-picnic stop of the Vaset-Gol day. The Vasskanten service house by Oset Fjellhotell at the lake's south-east end has changing facilities, charging and snacks, with SUP, canoe and motorboat hire on the lake and a lakeside sauna at the nearby Vasskanten cafe. The wider Golsfjellet plateau holds 20+ fishable lakes and rivers (season 1 Jun-10 Sep).
pois.detour: On route; the Mjolkevegen meets the lake at the Oset/Vasskanten end

pois.name: Gol Stave Church replica (Storoyne)
pois.description: Faithful 1994 copy of Gol's medieval stave church (the original, dated c. 1157-1216, was rescued in the 1880s and now stands at the Norwegian Folk Museum in Oslo), built by local craftsmen over roughly ten years of work. It stands at Storoyne riverside park in Gol centre with a stave-church architecture exhibition added in 2023; open daily 12:00-18:00 from 20 June to 20 August, grounds accessible anytime, free parking at Hallingparken.
pois.detour: On route, in Gol centre

pois.name: Torpo Stave Church
pois.description: Built c. 1192, the oldest building in Hallingdal and the valley's only surviving original stave church, famous for its painted 13th-century ceiling canopy depicting the legend of St Margaret. The nave was saved by the Society for the Preservation of Norwegian Ancient Monuments (Fortidsminneforeningen) when the chancel was demolished in 1880; open daily 10:00-18:00 from 1 June to 31 August 2026, guided tours bookable through Fortidsminneforeningen.
pois.detour: On route: Torpo village sits on the Gol-Al valley road ~15 km west of Gol, church 200 m off the road

pois.name: Al Bygdamuseum (Leksvol)
pois.description: Open-air museum of 30 historic buildings around the c. 1600 Leksvol farmhouse just above Al centre, covering Hallingdal farm life from valley farmhouse to mountain stol and cotter's plot. Open 1 July to 15 August 2026 (guided openings Mon/Tue/Thu 11:00-16:00 in peak weeks), so it falls inside your trip window.
pois.detour: ~1 km gentle climb west of Al centre, midway on the Gol-Geilo valley ride

pois.name: Strandafjorden bathing place
pois.description: The Hallingdal valley ride's best swimming stop: sandy shore, diving board and grassy picnic areas on Strandafjorden, 2 km west of Al beside Hallingdal Feriepark (Topcamp), signposted from Rv7 at Vangebrua. Note the lake is fed by the hydropower-regulated Hallingdalselva, so water levels can rise without warning.
pois.detour: On route, 2 km west of Al, a few hundred metres off Rv7

pois.name: Hol Old Church
pois.description: Hol's oldest church, 13th-century in origin: the apse survives from the original stave church, with Renaissance pulpit and font (1697) and a 1703 altarpiece; the parish church until the new Hol church at Hagafoss took over in 1924, still used for summer services. A quiet, genuinely old counterpoint to the busier stave churches.
pois.detour: ~3.5 km each way off Rv7 at Hagafoss on the Al-Geilo leg (about 7 km round trip)

pois.name: Hallingskarvet National Park
pois.description: 450 km2 national park (established 2006) around the 35 km Hallingskarvet massif, topped by Folarskardnuten at 1,933 m, the highest point in Buskerud. The wall of the Skarvet dominates the skyline for your whole Geilo-Haugastol ride; main summer entry is via Prestholtseter, with other access from Haugastol (Rv7) and Storestolen (road 50).
pois.detour: Visible from the route throughout; nearest entry point Prestholtseter, ~25 min from Geilo by summer toll road

pois.name: Prestholtskardet sherpa steps hike (from Prestholtseter)
pois.description: Signature Hallingskarvet hike: about 2,000 stone steps laid by sherpas in 2013 climb 3 km from Prestholtseter (1,242 m) to the Prestholtskardet gap at ~1,600 m, with views over Hallingdal and Hardangervidda (the Hardangerjokulen glacier on clear days); a loop variant (Prestholtrunden) returns behind the skard. Prestholtseter itself is a 1904 summer-farm cafe serving traditional food and home baking, open daily roughly early July to mid-August (then autumn weekends); the toll road normally opens around 1 July, so it should be open for a mid-July visit.
pois.detour: ~12 km / 20-25 min from Geilo up a summer-only toll road, cash or Vipps (a stiff but ridable gravel climb); hike 2-3 h, allow a half day

pois.name: Ustedalsfjorden Rundt with Fekjo cultural heritage park
pois.description: Easy 10 km loop around Geilo's lake, about 1 hour by bike or 2-3 on foot, with picnic spots and swimming along the way. On the south shore at Fekjo the trail passes a cluster of roughly 20 Viking-age burial mounds (excavated finds dated to the 900s, most of them women's graves) plus traces of iron-working and charcoal pits.
pois.detour: Starts and ends in Geilo centre; ideal rest-day or evening loop

pois.name: Geilojordet historic farmstead
pois.description: Free open-air farmstead in central Geilo with nine buildings from the 1700s-1900s gathered from farms in Hol and Al, plus a playground, farm animals and free storytelling theatre in summer. Summer cafe with traditional food (sour-cream porridge, pancakes) runs June to August, 11:00-16:00.
pois.detour: None; in Geilo centre by the church (Skurdalsvegen 2)

pois.name: Kikut - Hakkesetstolen and Bjodnahovda viewpoint
pois.description: From the Kikut cabin area south-east of Geilo, an easy family walk (partly wood-chipped and stroller-friendly) leads to Hakkesetstolen fjellstugu (summer food service) and on to the Bjodnahovda viewpoint, with views over Geilo, Ustedalsfjorden and across to Hallingskarvet. A low-effort way to get above the treeline if you skip the bigger Prestholt hike; also reachable from the Vestlia chairlift.
pois.detour: ~6 km by road from Geilo centre to the Kikut trailhead (Skurdalsvegen toward road 40), then a 2-3 km walk

pois.name: Haugastol - Rallarvegen kilometre zero
pois.description: Official start of the 80 km Rallarvegen at ~990 m: the navvies' construction road built for the Bergen Railway around the turn of the century (the road reached Haugastol by 1902), opened as a cycle route in 1974 and now Norway's most popular bike ride. Haugastol Turistsenter at the trailhead handles bike hire, service and luggage logistics before the high-mountain leg to Finse.
pois.detour: On route; this is where your middle section hands over to the Rallarvegen

poiGroups.verificationNotes: All 14 POIs are real; none removed. Verified against Wikipedia (en/no), stavechurch.com, golinfo.no, geilo.com, visitnorway.com, hallingparken.no, buskerudmuseene/WhichMuseum, plus Nominatim/Overpass geocoding and OSRM road routing. Exact coordinate matches (kept as-is): Lomen (Wikipedia 61.1347/8.9239), Torpo, Hol Old Church, Al Bygdamuseum/Leksvol, Prestholtseter, Storoyne, Haugastol, Hakkesetstolen, Geilojordet (80 m from Geilo kyrkje). Two pins moved: Tisleifjorden from the lake's far NW tip (60.875/8.839) to the SE Oset/Vasskanten end (60.8355/8.993) where the Mjolkevegen, swim facilities and rentals actually are (lake bbox per OSM: 60.836-60.877 / 8.832-9.018); Fekjo nudged ~700 m to the south-shore access at Flotagutu/Tuftebruvegen (60.526/8.157). Factual fixes: Prestholtseter cafe is seasonal (daily early Jul-mid Aug 10-16, autumn weekends), not year-round, and the toll road opens ~1 July; Prestholtskardet gap is ~1,600 m, not 1,700 (step count varies 2,000-2,500 by source); Torpo tours run by Fortidsminneforeningen (open daily 10-18, 1 Jun-31 Aug 2026), not Al tourist info, phone number removed, and stray 'Verified:' prefix deleted; Geilojordet cafe is Jun-Aug 11:00-16:00, not mid-Jun-early Sep 11-17; Gol replica took ~10 years of work, not 'ten person-years' (hours 20 Jun-20 Aug 12-18, 2023 exhibition and free Hallingparken parking confirmed by golinfo/hallingparken); Rallarvegen was built c. 1898-1902 for the Haugastol stretch (no.wikipedia: 1898-1909), not 'the 1890s', and 'Norway's most popular cycle route' is supported by visitnorway/Wikipedia (fjordnorway says 'one of'). Distance corrections from OSRM routing: Vaset-Lomen 22.6 km (was 18), Gol-Torpo 15.2 km (was 17), Hagafoss-Hol Old Church 3.4 km each way (was 2.5), Geilo-Prestholtseter 11.9 km (was 15), Geilo-Kikut 5.9 km (was 4-5); Al-Strandafjorden 2.1 km confirmed. Spelling: Bjodnehovda corrected to Bjodnahovda (Bjødnahovda). Verified superlatives/claims kept: Hallingskarvet 450 km2, est. 2006, Folarskardnuten 1,933 m highest in Buskerud, ~35 km massif, access via Prestholtseter/Haugastol/Storestolen; Torpo oldest building in Hallingdal and the valley's only surviving stave church; Lomen c.1192 with three carved portals, summer opening with NOK 130 admission (2025); Al museum 30 buildings, open 1 Jul-15 Aug 2026; Strandafjorden facilities and hydropower warning; Hol Old Church apse/1697/1703 and parish use until 1924; Fekjo ~20 mounds, 900s finds, mostly women's graves; Golsfjellet 20+ lakes, fishing 1 Jun-10 Sep; Stolsvidda farms Langestolen (cafe/store), Jaslangen and Trollhovd all exist in OSM, though 'working dairies' softened as unverifiable; Rallarvegen ~80 km Haugastol-Flam, cycle route since 1974. Residual uncertainty: Fekjo pin accurate to ~200 m; 2026 seasonal hours for Lomen, Geilojordet and Prestholtseter extrapolated from 2025/current listings, worth a re-check closer to departure.

pois.name: Hardangervidda National Park (northern edge)
pois.description: Mainland Norway's largest national park (3,422 sq km) on northern Europe's biggest mountain plateau, home to Europe's largest wild reindeer herd. The Rallarvegen between Haugastol and Finse runs along the plateau's northern rim (the formal park boundary lies a few km south of the trail): treeless tundra, lakes, and snow patches even in July. No fences or gates, the landscape IS the attraction. Watch for reindeer south of the trail around the Krækkja hut area.
pois.detour: 0 km, the Haugastol-Finse stage rides the plateau's northern rim just outside the park boundary

pois.name: Finse and Finse 1222 (Star Wars Hoth location)
pois.description: Highest station on the Bergen Line at 1,222 m, road-free, reachable only by rail or Rallarvegen. The Hoth claim is verified: Empire Strikes Back ice-planet scenes were shot on and around Hardangerjokulen from 5 March 1979, with cast and crew based at the Finse 1222 hotel (opened 1909). Hotel serves meals and beds if weather turns; the village hosts recurring Star Wars fan gatherings (Visit Hoth, Hoth Strikes Back).
pois.detour: 0 km, on route; overnight or lunch stop

pois.name: Rallarmuseet Finse (Navvy Museum)
pois.description: Museum at Finse station on the navvies who built the Bergen Line 1894-1909, with an authentic forge, construction barracks, and exhibitions on the fight against the snow with historic snow-clearing machinery. Open early July to mid-September; entry around NOK 40. The right context stop before riding the road those navvies built.
pois.detour: 0 km, at Finse station; 30-60 min visit

pois.name: Hardangerjokulen glacier (Blaisen/Midtdalsbreen views)
pois.description: Mainland Norway's sixth-largest glacier (ice cap to 1,863 m) dominating the skyline south of Finse. Best free views are from Finsevatnet lakeshore and the Rallarvegen west of Finse. The Blaisen and Midtdalsbreen tongues are walkable to the ice edge (do not go onto the ice unguided). This is the actual Hoth filming terrain.
pois.detour: Visible from route; ice-edge walk ~2 hr round trip on foot from Finse

pois.name: Guided glacier hike with Joklagutane
pois.description: Bookable in July: Joklagutane runs guided glacier walks on Hardangerjokulen (Blaisen) daily from early July, departing Finse railway station around 11:00. 6-7 hrs, ~16 km, guide/glacier equipment/insurance included, min age 12, good fitness needed. Book ahead at joklagutane.no (+47 993 31 222). Doing this means a full extra day at Finse, plan the itinerary around it.
pois.detour: Full day (6-7 hrs) from Finse; requires a rest/short-ride day

pois.name: Kleivabrua stone bridge, Kleivagjelet
pois.description: 35 m single-span stone bridge over the Kleivagjelet ravine between Hallingskeid and Myrdal, one of the hardest engineering problems on the line and the showpiece of navvy stonework. Exposed drop beside the trail; walking the bike past the ravine is normal if you dislike heights.
pois.detour: 0 km, on route

pois.name: Myrdalsberget 21 switchbacks (Kleva)
pois.description: The famous descent: 21 tight gravel hairpins dropping from Vatnahalsen off the Myrdalsberget cliff to the valley floor at Kardal, beside river and waterfall. Surface is rough, brakes overheat, and most riders walk at least some bends, fully loaded bikepacking rigs should. Top of the switchbacks gives the classic view down Flamsdalen. Flam Zipline (1,381 m, the Nordic region's longest) runs Vatnahalsen to Kardal if one of you wants the fast way down.
pois.detour: 0 km, on route; allow 30-45 min for the descent

pois.name: Kjosfossen waterfall
pois.description: 93 m main drop (225 m total) and the signature stop of the Flamsbana: trains pause 5 minutes at a platform reachable only by rail, where in summer a dancer plays the Huldra folklore spirit against the falls. From the bike route you get side views near Vatnahalsen; the full frontal view requires riding the train, an argument for taking the Flamsbana up from Flam as a day trip or for the outbound leg.
pois.detour: Partial view ~0 km from route; full view needs a Flamsbana ride

pois.name: Flamsbana (Flam Railway), Myrdal station
pois.description: 20 km branch line dropping 866 m from Myrdal to the fjord, gradient 1:18, the steepest standard-gauge adhesion railway in Europe and routinely called the world's most beautiful train ride. Takes bikes when space allows. Useful three ways: bail-out if weather or mechanicals hit, the Kjosfossen view, or a luggage-free joyride from Flam after you arrive.
pois.detour: 0 km, Myrdal is on route; ~1 hr ride if taken

pois.name: Flamsdalen valley descent
pois.description: The ~16 km run from Kardal to the fjord: a classic U-shaped valley with the Flamselvi river (salmon-green water), waterfalls off both walls, goat farms, and the railway threading above. Gradient eases after the switchbacks, so this is fast, scenic riding. Stock up on nothing, eat in Flam.
pois.detour: 0 km, this is the route

pois.name: Brekkefossen hike
pois.description: Best effort-to-view ratio in Flam: 2 km flat along the river from the village, then 578 stone steps climbing ~200 m to the Raokjen viewpoint beside the waterfall, looking over Flam village and down the Aurlandsfjord. 1.5-2.5 hrs round trip on foot, steep and slippery when wet. Ideal recovery-day leg stretch after the ride.
pois.detour: 2 km from Flam centre + 1.5-2.5 hr round trip on foot

pois.name: Flam Railway Museum
pois.description: Free museum beside Flam station covering the 20-year construction of the Flamsbana, with an original El9 locomotive (in service 1947-1983), navvy-era photos, and an audio guide. Good 30-45 min filler while waiting for a fjord boat or train, and it closes the loop on the railway history you have been riding through since Finse.
pois.detour: 0 km, in Flam centre; free entry

pois.name: Stegastein viewpoint
pois.description: 30 m steel-and-pine platform jutting from the mountainside 650 m above the Aurlandsfjord, the definitive aerial fjord view of the trip. By bike from Flam: ~10 km to Aurland then ~7-8 km of paved hairpins up the Aurlandsfjellet snow road, roughly 650 m of climbing, a stiff but rideable bonus climb for fit riders with unloaded bikes. Otherwise the electric shuttle/sightseeing bus from Flam runs several times daily in July (1-2 hr round trip, ~30 min at the top).
pois.detour: ~17 km + 650 m climb one way by bike (half day), or 1-2 hr by shuttle bus from Flam

pois.name: Otternes farm hamlet (Otternes Bygdetun)
pois.description: Clustered hamlet of 27 historic farm buildings from the 1700s, farmed in the traditional way until late in the 20th century, on a shelf above the Aurlandsfjord about 4 km from Flam on the old fjord road. In season open daily ~10:00-17:00 with guided tours four times a day and a small summer cafe, but staffing has been patchy in recent years: building interiors may be closed, and the yard and fjord view alone justify the stop. Access road is steep narrow gravel, fine by bike.
pois.detour: 4 km from Flam toward Aurland + short steep gravel climb; ~1 hr

poiGroups.verificationNotes: All 16 POIs are real and correctly located; no fabrications found. Verified against independent sources: Rallarmuseet (NOK 40, early July-mid Sept; geilo.com, visitnorway.com), Joklagutane glacier hikes (daily from early July, ~11:00 from Finse station, 6-7 hrs, ~16 km, min age 12, +47 993 31 222; visitnorway.com), Fagernut (1904, 1,310 m, mid-July opening 10:00-16:30; digitaltmuseum.no, haugastol.no), Kleivabrua 35 m span (norwaysbest.com), 21 hairpins on Myrdalsberget (cyclenorway.com, norwaysbest.com), Flam Zipline 1,381 m longest in the Nordics (vatnahalsen.no), Kjosfossen 93 m/225 m with Huldra (Wikipedia, world-of-waterfalls.com), Flamsbana 20 km/866 m/1:18 steepest standard-gauge in Europe, bikes carried space-permitting (Wikipedia, norwaysbest.com), Brekkefossen 578 steps to the Raokjen viewpoint (AllTrails, horizonrando.fr), Flam Railway Museum free with El9 locomotive (flamsbana.no), Stegastein 30 m platform 650 m above fjord with electric shuttle (visitbergen.com, fjordtours.com), Otternes 27 buildings 4 km from Flam (fjords.com), Naeroyfjord UNESCO 2005 2-hr cruise with bus return (flamtravelguide.com, lustrabaatane.no), Hoth filming from 5 March 1979 with crew at Finse 1222 and recurring fan events (themarysue.com, fanthatracks.com). Corrections applied: (1) Hardangervidda qualified as mainland Norway's largest park and 'skirts the park boundary' softened, the formal boundary lies a few km south of the Haugastol-Finse trail which rides the plateau's northern rim; (2) Hardangerjokulen qualified as mainland Norway's sixth-largest glacier; (3) Brekkefossen climb corrected from ~160 m to ~200 m; (4) Flamsdalen Kardal-to-fjord distance corrected from 18 km to ~16 km; (5) museum 'audio guides in 9 languages' unverifiable, softened to 'audio guide'; (6) Otternes 'oldest early 1600s' unsupported (sources say 1700s) and 'inhabited until the 1990s' softened, food/tour offer caveated due to 2025 reports of unstaffed periods; (7) Naeroyfjord vessels corrected, Legacy of the Fjords serves the Oslofjord, the Flam route uses Vision/Future of the Fjords, and the 'public ferry' alternative corrected to Lustrabaatane's seasonal ferry; (8) Stegastein coordinate nudged ~250 m to the Wikipedia-listed position. Coordinates for all other POIs sanity-checked against station positions (Finse 60.603/7.504, Myrdal 60.734/7.124) and valley geography; all fall in the correct valleys. Timing caveat retained: Fagernut may not yet be open at a ~14-15 July passage.

pois.name: Besseggen ridge hike (Gjende boat to Memurubu, hike back)
pois.description: The classic loop: boat Gjendesheim-Memurubu (20 min), hike ~14 km back over the ridge, ~1,100 m ascent, 6-8 h, demanding with light scrambling between Gjende (984 m) and Bessvatnet (1,373 m). 2026 boats run 6 Jun-19 Sept (daily departures through 13 Sept), up to 8 departures/day to Memurubu; take the earliest boat. Prices: one-way NOK 260 adult, same-day Gjendesheim-Memurubu return NOK 300, luggage NOK 100/piece. Pre-booking on gjende.no is required before arrival and tickets are non-refundable; July departures sell out, so book the dated 2026 sailing (already on sale, e.g. 17 Jul 2026 listings live) as soon as the itinerary is fixed. Bring a screenshot of the ticket QR. Bikes: no official bike storage published; quay parking is free for 2 h only, long-stay parking is at Reinsvangen 1.7 km away (shuttle starts 30 min before the first boat). Practical move: ask staff at the DNT Gjendesheim lodge to stow loaded bikes (staffed lodge, lock them regardless), or send panniers across on the boat (NOK 100, ask crew where labelled bags are left at the quay). Verified: gjende.no/en/time-tables/, gjende.no/en/some-practical-information/, visitnorway.com Besseggen guide.
pois.detour: Gjendesheim sits ~2 km off the Mjolkevegen/Valdresflye line (Rv51), so almost no extra riding, but it costs a full day; plan an overnight at Gjendesheim DNT or Bessheim

pois.name: Hardangerjokulen / Blaisen guided glacier walk from Finse
pois.description: Current operator is Joklagutane AS (booking site glaciertoursnorway.com). Roped glacier trek to the Blaisen arm of Hardangerjokulen: meets ~11:00 at the Finse station, back ~18:00; 6-7 h, 15-17 km round trip in unmarked high-mountain terrain. Includes guide, all glacier gear (crampons, harness, rope), insurance, small gift; min age 12; good fitness needed. Season 10 July-15 Sept, tours run essentially daily in summer, timed to Bergen Line trains. Price: web listings of NOK 650-690 trace back to 2018-era pages and are almost certainly stale; confirm current 2026 price and book at glaciertoursnorway.com (site intermittently slow/unreachable, confirmed during checking) or +47 993 31 222 / post@joklagutane.no. Timing note: season opens 10 July, riders hit Finse ~15-16 July, so it works, but book ahead. Verified: haugastol.no/en/glacier-tours (season 10 Jul-15 Sept, 11:00 meet, inclusions), geilo.com/en/activities/summer-activities/glacier-tour (15-17 km, min age 12).
pois.detour: Zero riding detour (Finse is on the Rallarvegen route) but it consumes a full day, 11:00-18:00; build in a second night at Finse 1222 or the DNT hut

pois.name: Stegastein viewpoint: ride the Bjorgavegen climb or shuttle from Flam
pois.description: Cantilevered platform 650 m above Aurlandsfjord; free, open 24/7, toilets on site. Ride option: from Aurlandsvangen the paved Bjorgavegen (fv243, the Aurlandsfjellet Snow Road) climbs ~9 km and ~630 m to the viewpoint (seven hairpins, busy with tourist cars); the full Aurlandsfjellet climb is 16.5 km / 1,295 m / 7.9% per climbfinder if you want the whole snow road. From Flam add ~9 km each way of flat fjord road to Aurland: budget ~38 km / 650 m+ round trip on unloaded bikes. Bus option: electric minibus tour from beside Flam railway station, 1.5 h round trip with ~25 min on the platform, from NOK 430/adult, runs year-round; book via norwaysbest.com or fjordtours.com. Verified: fjordtours.com (electric minibus, from NOK 430, 1.5 h, ~25 min at platform), climbfinder.com/en/climbs/aurlandsfjellet (16.5 km / 1,295 m / 7.9%, Stegastein at 9 km), coordinate databases (viewpoint at 60.9086, 7.2118).
pois.detour: Half day from Flam: ~3 h ride round trip from Flam (or 1.5 h by shuttle bus); coordinates given are the climb start in Aurlandsvangen, viewpoint itself at 60.9086, 7.2118

pois.name: Naeroyfjord cruise Flam-Gudvangen (Norway's Best)
pois.description: Premium electric sightseeing vessels (Future/Legacy of the Fjords) sail Flam-Gudvangen through Aurlandsfjord and the UNESCO-listed Naeroyfjord, ~2 h one way, multiple daily departures in July. Pricing is dynamic by date and operator: adult one-way NOK 520-595 typical (visitBergen lists NOK 550, child 4-15 NOK 275; peak-date Norway's Best fares can run higher); boat out + shuttle bus back ~NOK 760 total (bus leg ~30 min); full round trip by boat takes ~4.5-5 h and costs roughly double the single fare. Lustrabaatane also runs the leg Apr-Oct with a family ticket (~NOK 1,300 for 2+2). Book at norwaysbest.com (select date, leg, pax); July midday sailings fill, book a few days ahead. Good end-of-trip option: one-way and bus back is the time-efficient combo. Verified: flamtravelguide.com (one-way 520-595, round trip with bus 760, boat round trip 4.5-5 h), en.visitbergen.com (NOK 550 / child 275), norwaysbest.com.
pois.detour: Half day from Flam at trip end: ~2.5-3 h with bus return, ~4.5-5 h round trip by boat; no riding required

pois.name: Guided sea kayak on Aurlandsfjord from Flam (Njord)
pois.description: Njord Kayak Centre (seakayaknorway.com), beachside base a short walk from Flam station. Signature 3 h Fjord Paddle: NOK 1,235 adult / NOK 950 child, July departures daily 10:00, 12:00 and 15:00; shorter 2 h Family Fjord Paddle NOK 995 adult / NOK 825 child (08:30, 11:30, 17:00; runs 16 May-15 Sept, children over 5 welcome). All gear and guide included, no experience needed. Note: half-day paddles cover the Aurlandsfjord from Flam; reaching Naeroyfjord proper by kayak means a multi-day tour (e.g. Njord's 3-day Naeroyfjord kayak-camp tour, also on GetYourGuide). Book online at seakayaknorway.com or +47 913 26 628 / info@seakayaknorway.com; July slots fill, book ahead. Pairs well as a recovery-day activity after rolling into Flam. Verified: seakayaknorway.com/fjord-paddle-3-hour-guided-kayak-tour (NOK 1,235, daily 10:00/12:00/15:00 in July, family paddle NOK 995).
pois.detour: 3 h activity at the Flam finish line, zero riding detour; morning slot leaves the afternoon free for Stegastein or the fjord cruise

pois.name: Gjende boat (Gjendebatane) ticket logistics, Gjendesheim quay
pois.description: Standalone logistics entry for the boat that makes Besseggen work. 2026 season 6 Jun-19 Sept, daily service through 13 Sept (limited scheduled departures after, autumn extras announced later), up to 8 departures/day Gjendesheim-Memurubu (dated 2026 sailings already bookable online, e.g. mid-July listings live on gjende.no). Fares: Gjendesheim-Memurubu single NOK 260 adult / NOK 130 child, same-day return NOK 300 (child 150); Gjendesheim-Gjendebu single NOK 320, day return NOK 360; luggage NOK 100/item (buy labels on board or at the lodges); dogs free. Booking is required before arrival, tickets are non-refundable except in special circumstances, and July sailings sell out, so lock in dates early and screenshot the QR ticket. Parking: free max 2 h at the quay; long-stay at Reinsvangen 1.7 km with shuttle starting 30 min before the first boat (book shuttle online). Contact +47 61 23 92 93 / harald@gjende.no (no replies 21:30-07:00). Verified: gjende.no/en/time-tables/, gjende.no/en/some-practical-information/.
pois.detour: At Gjendesheim, ~2 km off the Valdresflye road; relevant only if doing Besseggen or sending luggage to Memurubu

poiGroups.verificationNotes: All 7 POIs verified as real; none removed. Coordinates all pass sanity checks: Gjendesheim quay (61.494, 8.81), Finse station (60.6017, 7.5041), Aurlandsvangen climb start (60.9058, 7.187) with Stegastein platform independently confirmed at 60.9086, 7.2118 via coordinate databases, Flam harbour/beach (60.863, 7.11), and Prestholtseter confirmed to four decimals against outdooractive (60.549396, 8.068072, 1,242-1,244 m). Confirmed against primary sources: Gjende 2026 season 6 Jun-19 Sept with daily service through 13 Sept, up to 8 daily Memurubu departures, fares NOK 260/300/360, luggage NOK 100, mandatory non-refundable pre-booking, 2 h quay parking, Reinsvangen 1.7 km (gjende.no); Joklagutane glacier tour season 10 Jul-15 Sept, 11:00 meet at Finse station, 6-7 h, 15-17 km, min age 12, gear/insurance included (haugastol.no, geilo.com); Njord Fjord Paddle NOK 1,235 adult with July departures 10:00/12:00/15:00 and Family Paddle NOK 995 at 08:30/11:30/17:00 (seakayaknorway.com); Stegastein electric minibus from NOK 430, 1.5 h, ~25 min at platform, year-round (fjordtours.com); Aurlandsfjellet climb 16.5 km / 1,295 m / 7.9% (climbfinder); Prestholt: ~2,000 sherpa steps (built 2013), Skarvsenden 1,705 m, loop ~6.5 km, toll NOK 40 cash/Vipps, road opens ~1 Jul, cafe daily early Jul-mid Aug. Corrections made: (1) glacier tour price: NOK 650-690 figures trace to 2018-era listings, flagged as stale rather than quotable, glaciertoursnorway.com timed out twice during checking so direct confirmation is genuinely needed; (2) fjord cruise: one-way corrected to NOK 520-595 typical (the NOK 665 figure kept only as a possible peak dynamic fare), boat+bus return corrected from ~775 to ~760, unverified ~NOK 950 boat-return figure replaced with 'roughly double the single fare', boat round trip duration corrected from ~4 h to 4.5-5 h; (3) Stegastein bus platform time tightened to ~25 min; (4) Reinsvangen shuttle 'every 30-60 min from 07:00' softened to the verified 'starts 30 min before the first boat'; (5) unverifiable 'left under tarp at the quay' luggage detail softened; (6) Prestholt cafe hours '10:00-16:00' not confirmed, softened to 'open daily early July to mid Aug'; (7) toll road opening softened from 'early July' to 'around 1 July' per outdooractive. Besseggen hike stats (14 km, ~1,100 m, 6-8 h, Gjende 984 m, Bessvatnet 1,373 m) match standard published figures.

logistics.gettingThere: Oslo S -> Vinstra is the Dovre line (Dovrebanen), operated by SJ Norge under the SJ Nord brand (line F6). Live Entur timetable for Thu 9 July 2026 (verified 11 June 2026): departures 07:50 (arr Vinstra 11:00), 09:50 (arr 13:01), then 13:50, 15:50, 17:50, plus a Vy RE10 at 18:28 (arr 21:40) and the night train at 22:49 (arr 02:25). Journey time ~3h10. Take the 07:50 to keep a half-day of riding toward Skåbu. Pricing is dynamic: non-flexible fares from NOK 299, realistically NOK 400-700 each in mid-July if booked a few weeks out. Critical constraint: bike capacity on the Dovre line is limited and bike spaces are pre-bookable (SJ does not publish an official per-train number; third-party sources cite ~5 spaces per set, and SJ itself only says space is 'limited'). Book both bikes as an add-on ('bicycle' under travellers) at sj.no or in the Vy app at the moment you buy passenger tickets; to add bikes to an existing ticket you must call SJ customer service. Bike ticket is 50% of a Standard Flexible (refundable) adult fare, min NOK 50 / max NOK 250 (so roughly NOK 150-250 Oslo-Vinstra). Note the 18:28 RE10 fallback is a Vy regional train with no bike reservation: you buy a bike ticket on board and the conductor decides if there is space, so do not rely on it with two loaded bikes.
logistics.gettingBack: Recommended: option (a), rail all the way, booked early. (a) Flåmsbana Flåm->Myrdal (R45, ~1h; departures 17 July 2026 verified live on Entur 11 June 2026: 07:20, 08:20, 12:10, 13:30, 16:00, 17:10), connecting at Myrdal to the Vy Bergen line (F4) to Oslo S, total 5h45-7h door to door (arrivals 13:07, 15:09, 19:07, 21:16, 22:27, 00:07; all six connections confirmed). Flåmsbana adult one-way from roughly NOK 399 on vy.no (dynamic, exact floor not independently verifiable; tourist-channel fares via Norway's Best run higher in peak season). Bike space on Flåmsbana is mandatory-reservation, 15 spaces per departure, and per vy.no can only be reserved during the summer season while Rallarvegen is open between Myrdal and Flåm; the bike fee follows Vy's standard rule (50% of the adult single, capped at NOK 275, so roughly NOK 200-275). Bikes must be unlocked, luggage off, and tagged with a bike tag from Flåm station, travelling on your own departure. Myrdal->Oslo on the Bergen line: dynamic fare ~NOK 500-900, plus flat NOK 275 summer bike fee (fixed regardless of distance, confirmed on vy.no), mandatory reservation, 15 spaces, bike must be labelled with destination/name/phone. Flåmsbana tickets open 120 days out; in mid-July (peak Rallarvegen season) book the day plans firm, the 15 bike slots per train are the binding constraint, not seats. (b) Norled express boat Flåm 15:30 -> Bergen (5.5h, daily 1 Apr-31 Oct), NOK 1,295 adult on the tourist cruise fare (all confirmed on norled.no); bikes travel free on Vestland express boats since 1 Oct 2024. Spectacular Sognefjord run, but pricier and you still need the Bergen->Oslo train (book NOK 275 bike space; night train exists). Only worth it if you want Bergen anyway. (c) Bus Flåm->Bergen (Nor-way Sognefjordekspressen NW420, ~3h, ~NOK 300-400): Nor-way's luggage policy explicitly lists NW420 as a route that does not accept bicycles, rule it out. No sensible option avoids booking bike spaces early.
logistics.bikeOnTrains: Three operators, three rule sets. SJ Norge (Dovre line): bike ticket 50% of Standard Flexible adult fare, min NOK 50 / max NOK 250; pre-bookable (and effectively essential) on the Dovre and Nordland lines; SJ publishes no per-train space count, only that space is limited (third-party sources cite ~5 per set); max bike length 175 cm, max tyre width 5 cm (bikes hang on hooks, so plus-size tyres are out); folding bikes free as luggage; lithium batteries out and in hand luggage, no on-board charging. Vy (Bergen line, Flåmsbana): fare 50% of adult single, min NOK 27 / max NOK 275, but a flat NOK 275 applies on the Bergen line in summer regardless of distance; reservation is mandatory on both the Bergen line (15 spaces) and Flåmsbana (15 spaces, only sold while Rallarvegen Myrdal-Flåm is open); Bergen line bikes must be labelled with destination, name, phone (labelling applies to the Bergen line only); e-bike batteries must be removed and carried as hand luggage; no cargo/Christiania bikes; tandems are normally banned but with two exceptions: Flåmsbana (hand the tandem in at Flåm early, it ships on an earlier departure) and dedicated Bergen line summer departures with bicycle carriages, one bike ticket per tandem; Vy's 175 cm / 5 cm hook limits apply too. Flåmsbana additionally requires the bike unlocked, stripped of luggage, and tagged (tags at Flåm station), travelling on your own departure. Bike trailers: allowed if space, same fee as a bike, book via customer service. Universal catch: Vy confirms bikes are not carried on Bergen line rail-replacement buses and discourages them on all other replacement services, so check for planned engineering works (common on the Bergen line in summer) a few days before each leg.
logistics.julyConditions: Plan for everything from 0C sleet to 20C sun in the same week. Finse (1,222 m) in July: average high 12.6C, low 5.5C, the warmest month; rain on roughly half of days; snowfall still occurs ~2 days in an average July. Valdresflye (road summit 1,389 m) and Stølsvidda are comparable: typically 5-15C, exposed, and wind is the bigger factor than rain, there is no shelter for long stretches. Haugastøl's official advice is windproof and waterproof layers as mandatory kit; add warm gloves and a buff for the Finse-Myrdal day. Daylight is a non-issue: ~19-20 hours in mid-July at this latitude (sun roughly 04:30-22:45, no real darkness), so late arrivals into camp are fine and you never need to ride in the dark. Mosquitoes: expect them at camp near marshy ground and still water on Stølsvidda, Valdresflye and around Finse's lakes on calm evenings; breeze at altitude usually keeps them down while riding. Bring repellent and make sure the tent mesh is intact; it is an annoyance, not a plague year-type problem at these elevations.
logistics.rallarvegen: Timing works, but it is the tightest variable in the plan. Official picture: the lower Myrdal-Flåm section opens earliest (the official report says Vatnahalsen-Flåm typically opens early May and Flåmsbana's site calls the low section snow-free June-October); Haugastøl-Finse usually opens second half of June; the high Finse-Hallingskeid stretch historically opens early July (Flåmsbana's site says snowmelt normally allows cycling over the high mountains Haugastøl-Myrdal from mid-July). As of the official status report dated 8 June 2026 (re-verified live on haugastol.no, 11 June 2026): ~17 km plowed from Haugastøl, Hallingskeid-Vatnahalsen and Vatnahalsen-Flåm fully open (some rocky, washed-out sections after the thaw), Finse-Hallingskeid still snowbound, with more snow than normal for the time of year, and not recommended; full route forecast passable in early July 2026. The above-normal snow on the high section makes the pre-departure check non-optional. Riding it around 15-16 July you should still be fine, but expect residual snow patches with short hike-a-bike near Fagernut even after official opening. Check before committing: the Rallarvegen Report at haugastol.no/en/rallarvegen-report (rallarvegen.com redirects there) is the live status page with webcams; Finse 1222 hotel (summer season from early July) and the bike-rental opening dates are good secondary signals: Flåm 2 May, Haugastøl 22 June, Finse 3 July in 2026. If Finse-Hallingskeid is still flagged red when you arrive, the bail-out is the Bergen line train Finse->Myrdal or Finse->Hallingskeid (bike space rules apply) and ride from there. VERIFICATION SUMMARY (11 June 2026): all train times re-checked against the live Entur API and confirmed exact for both 9 and 17 July 2026; SJ, Vy and Flåmsbana bike rules confirmed against sj.no, vy.no and flamsbana.no; Rallarvegen status confirmed against the live haugastol.no report; Norled price/season/free-bikes, Nor-way NW420 no-bikes rule, DNT NOK 860, and the Jotunheimvegen still-closed notice all confirmed at source. Two corrections: the '5 bike spaces per Dovre line train' figure is third-party, not published by SJ; Vy tandems on Flåmsbana ship on an earlier departure rather than 'special arrangement'. One claim not independently verifiable: the NOK 399 Flåmsbana fare floor (dynamic pricing).
practicalNotes: Sunday 12 July: regular supermarkets (Kiwi, Rema 1000, Coop Extra) are closed by law. Open: small-format 'Brustadbu' shops under 100 m2 (Joker, some Bunnpris), kiosks, and petrol-station shops under 150 m2; designated tourist areas can have more open. Beitostolen, Gol and Geilo are resort towns with Joker-type and petrol-station options, but do the big shop on Saturday 11 July. No take-home alcohol sales on Sundays anywhere.

Wild camping (allemannsretten, Outdoor Recreation Act s.9): free on uncultivated land, minimum 150 m from inhabited houses and cabins, max 2 nights in one spot without landowner permission (longer allowed in remote high mountains), leave no trace. Some Fjord Norway hotspots have local restrictions; the Flam valley floor is farmland, use the campsite there.

Open-fire ban 15 April-15 September in or near forest and other vegetated land (fire prevention regulation s.3). Camping stoves are fine; fires only where it obviously cannot spread (bare rock by water), and local total bans can apply in dry spells.

Camping gas (standard screw-thread canisters) is stocked by Norwegian sports shops: Intersport Beitostolen (centre, also open Sundays 10-16), Intersport Gol (Kremmartunet, Sentrumsvegen 116) and Lie Sport og Fritid/Sport 1 (Sentrumsvegen 110), Intersport Geilo and Sport 1 Geilo / Geilo Sport. You cannot fly with canisters, so buy in Oslo or Beitostolen on day 1.

Bike workshops/spares: Intersport Beitostolen (full sykkelverksted), Intersport Gol and Lie Sport Gol (both have repair workshops), Intersport Geilo / Geilo Sport, Haugastol Turistsenter (Rallarvegen rental hub, well stocked with brake pads, tubes, tools), Finse bike rental (from 3 July 2026) and Flam Bike Rental. Carry spare brake pads regardless: the Myrdal descent eats them.

Jotunheimvegen (Skabu-Bygdin, 45 km gravel, on your day 1-2): private toll road with booths at Bygdin and Finnbole; published tariffs cover motor vehicles (motorcycle NOK 60 in 2025, cars more); no bicycle tariff is published and cyclists pass free in practice, but verify at jotunheimvegen.no. More important: it normally opens only in the last week of June (confirmed 11 June 2026: the site still shows the 2025 season-closed notice), and there are no services or food for the full 45 km. Check 'Oppdatert veginfo' on jotunheimvegen.no the week before.

Payments: Norway is effectively cashless. Visa/Mastercard contactless works everywhere including campsites, huts and the smallest kiosks. Some self-service DNT huts assume Vipps (Norwegian-only app) but all take card details on paper. Carry no or minimal cash.

DNT membership (adult NOK 860, confirmed current price on dnt.no): gives ~NOK 100-150/night discounts at staffed huts (Finsehytta at Finse, Gjendesheim, Fondsbu). For a tent-based trip with at most one or two hut nights, it does not pay back; skip it unless you decide to sleep at Finsehytta and Gjendesheim both.

Emergency numbers: 112 police, 113 ambulance, 110 fire. Install the 'Hjelp 113' app, it transmits your GPS position to dispatch. Mobile coverage is good along most of the route; patchiest between Finse and Hallingskeid.

Gjendesheim/Bygdin: no formally published bike or luggage storage. Gjendesheim (DNT, at the Besseggen trailhead ~8 km off Valdresflye) routinely accommodates cyclists leaving bikes while hiking, but confirm by phone ahead. At Bygdin, the M/B Bitihorn boat crosses the lake to Eidsbugarden in summer (passengers and bikes, no vehicles) and Bygdin Hoifjellshotell is the practical place to ask about leaving anything.

Water and food gaps: stream water above ~1,000 m is generally drinkable (official Mjolkevegen FAQ still advises treating it; a lightweight filter settles the argument). Carry a full day of food for both the Jotunheimvegen stretch and Haugastol-Flam: between Haugastol and Flam the only food stops are Finse (hotel/DNT hut, from early July) and Cafe Rallaren at Myrdal.

gaps.description: No en-route water/food detail for the two remote plateau crossings. Skabu to Bygdin via Jotunheimvegen is ~50-60 km of high gravel with Haugseter Fjellstue as the only stop, and its July opening days/serving hours for passers-by are unconfirmed. Same for the Stolsvidda day (Beitostolen-Vaset, 67 km): the summer-farm cafes listed (Langestolen, Lisjordetstolen etc.) often open only limited days/hours in early-mid July, and toll-road payment (Jotunheimvegen and Panoramavegen bomveg, Vipps/card) isn't covered. Riders need confirmed open cafes, reliable stream/water points, and a carry plan if everything is shut.

Snow regularly covers the Finse-Hallingskeid section into mid-July (Finse bike services only open ~3 July per haugastol.no/en/rallarvegen-report), so a 16 July full ride is plausible but not guaranteed. Missing: how to check live trail status the week before, the fallback of taking the Bergensbanen local train Finse-Myrdal or Hallingskeid with bikes (very limited bike spaces, reservation rules), Flamsbana's bike policy from Myrdal, and holdover options at Finse if weather pins them at 1,222 m.

gaps.description: No date-specific checks for 9-17 July 2026. Two concrete risks: (1) Vy/Bane NOR routinely runs summer track works on Dovrebanen and Bergensbanen in July, and rail-replacement buses generally refuse bikes; this could break both the Oslo-Vinstra arrival on 9 July and the return from Flam/Myrdal on 17 July. (2) Vinjerock festival runs 16-19 July 2026 at Eidsbugarden on Bygdin; the build-up days (14-16 July) load the M/B Bitihorn boat and Bygdin/Beitostolen-area beds, marginal overlap with their days 2-5 but worth confirming.

gaps.description: Camper-specific coverage is thin at the high/expensive stops. Sleep counts exist per town but not: legal freecamp rules and concrete tent spots at Gjendesheim (Jotunheimen NP edge, designated camping by the lodge), Finse (exposed, windy, allemannsretten distance rules near the station/hotel), and Flam (no wild camping in the village; Flam Camping fills in July). Food angle for tent nights: Geilo is the last full supermarket before the Rallarvegen, Haugastol has only a kiosk/cafe and Finse no shop at all, and whether Finsehytta/Finse 1222 serve dinner to non-guests without pre-booking is unanswered.
